## pqac-00000000

From text 'bahr2018collaborativeevaluationof pages 5-7'.

Matthew N. Bahr, David B. Damon, Simon D. Yates, Alexander S. Chin, J. David Christopher, Samuel Cromer, Nicholas Perrotto, Jorge Quiroz, and Victor Rosso. Collaborative evaluation of commercially available automated powder dispensing platforms for high-throughput experimentation in pharmaceutical applications. Organic Process Research & Development, 22:1500-1508, Oct 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.oprd.8b00259, doi:10.1021/acs.oprd.8b00259. This article has 63 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports comparative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, and dispense-time/throughput) for commercial powder dispensers (Mettler‑Toledo Quantos QB5, Chemspeed SWING/SDU, Unchained Laboratories Freeslate/Junior). Mean masses were generally comparable across systems, but accuracy (% error) improved as target mass increased (2 → 10 → 50 mg). Precision degrades markedly at low targets: 2 mg dispenses showed 190%–680% higher %RSD versus 10 mg and 260%–1700% higher %RSD versus 50 mg, indicating poor precision for low-mass dispensing. Throughput (dispense time) increased with target mass; Chemspeed SWING/SDU had the shortest dispense times overall. Dispense-time data capture was incomplete for some installations (e.g., only 21.4% of Chemspeed dispenses had time data; Mettler‑Toledo QB5 times recorded for only two of three systems). Performance depended strongly on powder type (e.g., L‑proline and thiamine HCl easier; fumed silica, limestone, sodium chloride most challenging) and dispense head choice. The report highlights a gap in accurately dispensing low target masses and the importance of optimal head selection. The excerpt does not provide pricing information for any systems.

The excerpt reports experimental characterization of a Coperion K-Tron KT-20 C-gearbox loss-in-weight feeder: 14 materials, four screw types (coarse auger, fine auger, coarse concave, fine concave) and six feed rates were run for a total of 336 experiments. Key findings: motor-driven screw speed (controlled by feedback) sets mass feed rate and an effective motor operating range was observed between 20% and 90% of drive command; the authors define a "feed factor" as the maximum mass flow for a material/feeder/screw at 100% drive. A correlation was found between conditioned bulk density (material packing) and the feed factor, enabling prediction of maximum flow per screw type from powder characterization. The text also references methods evaluating a K-Tron KT-35. ## pqac-00000011

From text 'schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 7-8'.

M. Schneck, M. Horn, Matthias Schmitt, C. Seidel, G. Schlick, and G. Reinhart. Review on additive hybrid- and multi-material-manufacturing of metals by powder bed fusion: state of technology and development potential. Progress in Additive Manufacturing, 6:881-894, Aug 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2, doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2. This article has 132 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies multiple challenges and gaps relevant to powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material AM alloy discovery. Key challenges include powder cross-contamination (worse at higher deposition rates), layer-bonding difficulties between different materials, powder deposition inaccuracies, and the need to adapt process parameters locally at material transition zones. Data-preparation limitations are important: coater-based MM workflows use separate STL files per volumetric section and nozzle concepts require bespoke CNC code, making data prep error-prone and preventing efficient graded-material definitions; advanced volumetric data formats (AMF, 3MF, FAV, SVX) are available but not widely implemented. Post-process powder reconditioning is problematic because cross-contamination forces sorting; manual ferromagnetic separation showed highly variable purities, automated sorting performed worse, while sieving and sedimentation delivered good purities for some cases (e.g., sieving >97.6% part.%, sedimentation separable for W vs Al/Ti). Demonstrated deposition/dosing concepts include holohedral, nozzle-based, and pressure-based masked deposition, but contamination levels (e.g., 14–40 wt.% for holohedral overflows) and device-dependent contamination have not been fully characterized. Monitoring methods (active thermography, CCD, spectrometers/photodetectors) can detect contaminants, and initial ML approaches exist for predicting scanning parameters, but comprehensive automated mixing/dosing systems and standardized solutions for high-throughput, low-contamination multi-material metal AM remain limited.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'wei2021recentprogressand pages 3-5'.

Chao Wei and Lin Li. Recent progress and scientific challenges in multi-material additive manufacturing via laser-based powder bed fusion. Virtual and Physical Prototyping, 16:347-371, May 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/17452759.2021.1928520, doi:10.1080/17452759.2021.1928520. This article has 320 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies key challenges for multi-material powder dispensing in L-PBF: unmelted powder retention in the bed causes cross-contamination when attempting to deposit dissimilar powders in the same layer, necessitating cleaning between depositions; blade-based recoaters cannot reliably deposit different powders on the same layer and repeated scraping can damage soft blade tips, degrading surfaces. Ultrasonic dispensing offers micron-scale feed precision and patterning capability but is limited by very low point-by-point throughput and requires improvement in layer thickness uniformity. Hybrid approaches (blade + ultrasonic) combine a main blade spread with ultrasonic dispensers for small-volume materials and employ micro-vacuum suction to remove excess unmelted powder to reduce contamination. Engineering fixes to ultrasonic dispensers include adding a miniature vibration motor to stabilize powder flow and creating arrays of dispensers (six-unit arrays) to print spatial FGMs. Electrophotographic/electrostatic methods (Aerosint dual-toner selective spreading) and dual-powder recoater concepts have been demonstrated for complex SS–copper patterns. These represent automated powder dosing/multiplexing strategies, but throughput, cross-contamination control, and uniform layer formation remain gaps for high-throughput alloy discovery workflows.

## pqac-00000013

From text 'schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 5-7'.

URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2, doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2. This article has 132 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies several gaps and limitations in powder dispensing for multi-material PBF relevant to high-throughput alloy discovery. Reported nozzle-based feed rates translate to roughly 0.4–4.1 mm3/s per nozzle (examples: 5–15 mm/s travel giving 10–30 mg/s; up to 32 mg/s for 316L), and powder/part feature sizes range from ~85 µm powder tracks up to millimeter-scale deposited lines (0.8–1.6 mm). Nozzle-based concepts are at MRL 4–5, while masked/photoconductor approaches are earlier (MRL ~2) and lack published deposition rates. Pressure-based masked systems (suction drums with valve matrices) can deposit longer powder lines quickly (demo: 150 mm in ~8 s), suggesting higher throughput but feature-resolution limitations. Key challenges include limited deposition rate data for some methods, relatively coarse minimum feature sizes for deposited powder lines, early technology maturity, and integration gaps: material distribution/design tools, local material-property definition, software limits, process monitoring, and understanding of metallurgical mixing/heat-affected zones during in-situ alloying. The excerpt documents existing deposition strategies—nozzle arrays, pressure-based drums, photoconductor masking and suction/coater combinations—but does not detail automated lab-scale powder mixing/dosing hardware solutions beyond these process concepts.

## pqac-00000014

From text 'wei2021recentprogressand pages 1-3'.

URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/17452759.2021.1928520, doi:10.1080/17452759.2021.1928520. This article has 320 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses powder-deposition approaches and limitations for multi-material L-PBF. Common implementations use dual powder containers and a blade recoater to switch powders between layers, enabling vertical bimetallic builds but producing sharp, abrupt interfaces that concentrate stress and risk delamination. An improved blade-based dual-powder recoater with an in situ powder-mixing function has been demonstrated to produce vertical functionally graded (Ti6Al4V/In718) structures, indicating one route toward gradient interfaces. However, blade-based spreading as described cannot deposit different powders within the same build layer (i.e., lateral material patterning) — a key gap for high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery that requires rapid, spatially-resolved dosing and mixing. The text also notes L-PBF uses very fine powders, small laser spots, and thin layers (powder: 10–50 μm; spot: 50–80 μm; layer thickness: <100 μm), implying stringent requirements on powder-dosing precision and homogeneity for multi-material dispensing. Overall, the excerpt highlights existing solutions (dual containers, blade recoaters, in situ mixing modification) and their limits (no same-layer multi-powder deposition, sharp interfaces), which are critical challenges for automated, high-throughput multi-material alloy exploration.

Jiri Zegzulka, Daniel Gelnar, Lucie Jezerska, Rostislav Prokes, and Jiri Rozbroj. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. Scientific Reports, Dec 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The FT4 (Freeman Technology, Tewkesbury, UK) rotary shear module is used to measure friction parameters and flowability (AIFE and flow index ffc) of metal powders. Its measurement principle uses a vessel containing the sample and a shear head whose blades sink into the powder; the head applies normal stress, moves downward until a stable pressure is achieved, then rotates slowly to induce shear. A shear plane forms just below the blade ends; because the powder bed resists rotation, shear stress in the measuring plane rises until slippage occurs, and the maximum transferred shear stress at slippage is recorded. In this study AIFE was measured at a consolidated normal stress of 10,000 Pa and the FT4 software was used to derive the flow index ffc. Compared with the other shear cells used, the FT4 had the smallest shear cell area (1879 mm2) but the highest bed level (44.5 mm), indicating differences in sample volume and geometry that can affect measured shear strength and ffc.


---

## pqac-00000019

From text 'zegzulka2020characterizationandflowability pages 11-14'.

Jiri Zegzulka, Daniel Gelnar, Lucie Jezerska, Rostislav Prokes, and Jiri Rozbroj. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. Scientific Reports, Dec 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes how the Freeman Technology FT4 powder rheometer prepares and measures metal powder samples: an impeller penetrates through the entire powder bed during sample preparation, loosening (aerating) and standardizing the sample before shear measurements. This built-in loosening/aeration step can lead FT4 to report smaller AIFE (angle of internal friction equivalent) values for some powders (e.g., Zn: FT4 AIFE 26.5° ±0.4) but larger values for others (e.g., Ti: FT4 AIFE 45.6° ±1.9), likely influenced by cell design differences. The FT4 provides AIFE and flow index (ffc) outputs comparable to PFT and RST devices; according to ffc the FT4 classifies many tested metal powders as free-flowing and produces similar flow-mode assignments to PFT. However, the excerpt notes larger variance for certain materials (notably titanium and molybdenum), indicating FT4 may be less suitable or less stable for those powders, and it cautions that ffc sensitivity alone may be insufficient for distinguishing flow regimes. Overall, FT4 capability highlights sample aeration/standardization, shear/ AIFE measurement, and ffc-based flow classification, with material-dependent variability.

## pqac-00000020

From text 'brika2020influenceofparticle pages 5-8'.

Salah Eddine Brika, Morgan Letenneur, Christopher Alex Dion, and Vladimir Brailovski. Influence of particle morphology and size distribution on the powder flowability and laser powder bed fusion manufacturability of ti-6al-4v alloy. Additive manufacturing, 31:100929, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addma.2019.100929, doi:10.1016/j.addma.2019.100929. This article has 543 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The FT4 powder rheometer (Freeman Technology) is presented as a more sensitive, instrumented method than funnel tests for detailed powder rheology in metal AM. The study used five FT4 testing modes: dynamic flow, aeration, permeability, compressibility, and shear. Dynamic flow measures torque from a rotating blade in a filled vessel and reports metrics including basic flow energy (BFE, energy in the 7th downward repetition), conditioned bulk density (ρc), and specific energy (SE, energy during upward traverse). The aeration test introduces air at varying velocities to a loosely packed bed and records resistance; software outputs include aeration energy (AE). Compressibility is measured with a vented piston applying increasing compressive load to get powder bed volume change and a compressibility index (CI = compressed density / ρc). The permeability test introduces air at the column base under increasing compressive loads, monitoring air pressure to calculate pressure drop (PD). Shear testing evaluates stresses needed to initiate flow under consolidation and yields metrics such as the cohesion coefficient (c). Measurements were replicated three times with averages and standard deviations reported. Manufacturer documentation is referenced for further FT4 outputs and operational details.

## pqac-00000021

From text 'zegzulka2020characterizationandflowability pages 14-15'.

Jiri Zegzulka, Daniel Gelnar, Lucie Jezerska, Rostislav Prokes, and Jiri Rozbroj. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. Scientific Reports, Dec 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports several FT4-specific measurement details relevant to metal powder characterization. FT4 produced, in most cases, the lowest flow factor of cohesion (ffc) among the compared instruments; exceptions were Zn and Mo. The reported slip-surface (shear cross-section) area for FT4 is 1879 mm2 (smaller than PFT and RST), and the FT4 specimen column height used here was 44.5 mm (RST 19.3 mm, PFT 9 mm). These geometry differences correlate with FT4 giving the highest measured unconfined yield strength (σc) for the same normal loads, indicating that a smaller shear cross-section and higher column increase σc and reduce apparent flowability. Tests were run at normal loads of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 kPa; differing σc values are required when ffc differs across devices even with the same normal loads. The authors note that ffc depends on slip-surface size and can be affected by machine settings (e.g., set normal tension) and particle deformation. They also caution that the flow index’s sensitivity alone may be insufficient to classify flow regimes, and other particle properties (size, shape, roughness) and instrument parameters influence results.

## pqac-00000022

From text 'zegzulka2020characterizationandflowability pages 10-11'.

Jiri Zegzulka, Daniel Gelnar, Lucie Jezerska, Rostislav Prokes, and Jiri Rozbroj. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. Scientific Reports, Dec 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports use of the Freeman Technology FT4 as one of three shear-cell-style devices (RST, PFT, FT4) to determine AIFE (effective angles related to internal friction) for metal powders. FT4 produced the largest variations in measured AIFE values among the instruments tested, likely reflecting methodological differences. The FT4 measurement involves an impeller (shear head) that penetrates the entire powder bed and during standardised sample preparation the powder bed is loosened as part of the measurement. The text notes a 1 mm clearance between the glass cylinder and the measuring impeller where larger particles (e.g., titanium) can wedge, increasing resistance and shear stress—indicating FT4 sensitivity to particle size and shape. SEMs correlated particle morphology (spherical, oval, smooth vs. complex shapes) with AIFE outcomes across instruments including FT4, and powders were grouped into lower/middle/higher AIFE areas (e.g., 316L, Zn, Sn in the lower area). Overall, the excerpt highlights FT4's role in AIFE measurement, its sensitivity to particle geometry and size effects, and that its measurement method can cause powder bed loosening and greater spread in reported angles.

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder
Valid Text Names:
'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 1-2', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 2-3', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 3-4', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 4-5', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 5-7', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 7-11', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 11-12', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 12-13', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 13-13'

BibTex:
@article{fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof,
    author = "Fathollahi, Sara and Sacher, Stephan and Escotet-Espinoza, M. Sebastian and DiNunzio, James and Khinast, Johannes G.",
    title = "Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "AAPS PharmSciTech",
    volume = "21",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "8",
    issn = "1530-9932"
}


Abstract:
Highly potent active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and low-dose excipients, or excipients with very low density, are notoriously hard to feed with currently available commercial technology. The micro-feeder system presented in this work is capable of feeding low-dose rates of powders with different particle sizes and flow properties. Two different grades of lactose, di-calcium phosphate, croscarmellose sodium, silicon dioxide, a spray-dried intermediate, and an active ingredient were studied to vary material properties to test performance of the system. The current micro-feeder system is a volumetric feeder combined with a weighing balance at the outlet that measures feeder output rates. Feeding results are shown as a so-called “displacement-feed factor” curve for each material. Since the powder mass and volume are known in the micro-feeder system, in this work, we characterized an observed density variation during processing via a “displacement-feed factor” profile for each of the fed powders. This curve can be later used for calibrating the system to ensure an accurate, constant feed rate and in addition predicting feeding performance for that material at any feed rate. There is a relation between powder properties and feeding performance. Powders with finer particles and higher compressibility show densification during their feeding process. However, powders with larger particles and lower compressibility show both “densification” and “powder bed expansion,” which is the manifestation of dilation and elastic recovery of particles during the micro-feeding process. Through the application of the displacement-feed factor, it is possible to provide precise feeding accuracy of low-dose materials.

    title = "Process Design of Continuous Powder Blending Using Residence Time Distribution and Feeding Models",

    pages = "1119",
    month = "Nov",

    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics12111119",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "11",
    issn = "1999-4923"
}


Abstract:
The present paper reports a thorough continuous powder blending process design of acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) and microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) based on the Process Analytical Technology (PAT) guideline. A NIR-based method was applied using multivariate data analysis to achieve in-line process monitoring. The process dynamics were described with residence time distribution (RTD) models to achieve deep process understanding. The RTD was determined using the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) as a tracer with multiple designs of experiment (DoE) studies to determine the effect of critical process parameters (CPPs) on the process dynamics. To achieve quality control through material diversion from feeding data, soft sensor-based process control tools were designed using the RTD model. The operation block model of the system was designed to select feasible experimental setups using the RTD model, and feeder characterizations as digital twins, therefore visualizing the output of theoretical setups. The concept significantly reduces the material and instrumental costs of process design and implementation.

Abstract:
In order to be at pace with the market requirements of solid dosage forms and regulatory standards, a transformation towards systematic processing using continuous manufacturing (CM) and automated model-based control is being thought through for its fundamental advantages over conventional batch manufacturing. CM eliminates the key gaps through the integration of various processes while preserving quality attributes via the use of process analytical technology (PAT). The twin screw extruder (TSE) is one such equipment adopted by the pharmaceutical industry as a substitute for the traditional batch granulation process. Various types of granulation techniques using twin screw extrusion technology have been explored in the article. Furthermore, individual components of a TSE and their conjugation with PAT tools and the advancements and applications in the field of nutraceuticals and nanotechnology have also been discussed. Thus, the future of granulation lies on the shoulders of continuous TSE, where it can be coupled with computational mathematical studies to mitigate its complications.

Abstract:
Purpose In continuous manufacturing (CM), the material traceability and process dynamics can be investigated by residence time distribution (RTD). Many of the unit operations used in the pharma industry were characterized by dead time–dominated RTD. Even though feasible and proper feedback control is one of the many advantages of CM, its application is challenging in these cases. This study aims to develop a feedback control, implementing the RTD in a Smith predictor control structure in a continuous powder blender line. Methods Continuous powder blending was investigated with near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR), and the blending was controlled through a volumetric feeder. A MATLAB GUI was developed to calculate and control the concentration of the API based on the chemometric evaluation of the spectra. The programmed GUI changed the feeding rate based on the proportional integral derivative (PID) and the Smith predictor, which implemented the RTD of the system. The control structures were compared even on a system with amplified dead time. Results In this work, the control structure of the Smith control was devised by utilizing the RTD of the system. The Smith control was compared to a classic PI control structure on the normal system and on an increased dead time system. The Smith predictor was able to reduce the response time for various disturbances by up to 50%, and the dead time had a lower effect on the control. Conclusions Implementing the RTD models in the control structure improved the process design and further expanded the wide range of applications of the RTD models. Both control structures were able to reduce the effect of disturbances on the system; however, the Smith predictor presented more reliable and faster control, with a wider space for control tuning.

'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 1-2', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 2-3', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 3-5', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 5-6', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 6-8', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 8-10', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 10-12', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 10-10', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 12-13', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 13-14', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 14-15', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 15-16', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 16-16', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 16-17', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 17-18', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 18-19', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 19-21', 'bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach pages 21-22'

@article{bachawala2024rationalfunctionbasedapproach,
    author = "Bachawala, Sunidhi and Lagare, Rexonni B. and Delaney, Abigail B. and Nagy, Zoltan K. and Reklaitis, Gintaras V. and Gonzalez, Marcial",
    title = "Rational Function-Based Approach for Integrating Tableting Reduced-Order Models with Upstream Unit Operations: Dry Granulation Case Study",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "Pharmaceuticals",
    volume = "17",
    pages = "1158",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.3390/ph17091158",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/ph17091158",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "9",
    issn = "1424-8247"
}


Abstract:
We present a systematic and automatic approach for integrating tableting reduced-order models with upstream unit operations. The approach not only identifies the upstream critical material attributes and process parameters that describe the coupling to the first order and, possibly, the second order, but it also selects the mathematical form of such coupling and estimates its parameters. Specifically, we propose that the coupling can be generally described by normalized bivariate rational functions. We demonstrate this approach for dry granulation, a unit operation commonly used to enhance the flowability of pharmaceutical powders by increasing granule size distribution, which, inevitably, negatively impacts tabletability by reducing the particle porosity and imparting plastic work. Granules of different densities and size distributions are made with a 10% w/w acetaminophen and 90% w/w microcrystalline cellulose formulation, and tablets with a wide range of relative densities are fabricated. This approach is based on product and process understanding, and, in turn, it is not only essential to enabling the end-to-end integration, control, and optimization of dry granulation and tableting processes, but it also offers insight into the granule properties that have a dominant effect on each of the four stages of powder compaction, namely die filling, compaction, unloading, and ejection.

## 2. Multimaterial powder bed fusion techniques
Valid Text Names:
'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 1-2', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 2-4', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 4-5', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 5-7', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 7-9', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 9-11', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 11-13', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 13-15', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 15-16', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 16-16', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 16-18', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 18-19', 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 18-18'

    author = "Mehrpouya, Mehrshad and Tuma, Daniel and Vaneker, Tom and Afrasiabi, Mohamadreza and Bambach, Markus and Gibson, Ian",
    title = "Multimaterial powder bed fusion techniques",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "Rapid Prototyping Journal",
    volume = "28",
    pages = "1-19",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014",
    publisher = "Emerald",
    issue = "11",
    issn = "1355-2546"
}


Abstract:

Purpose
This study aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of the art in powder bed fusion (PBF) techniques for additive manufacturing of multiple materials. It reviews the emerging technologies in PBF multimaterial printing and summarizes the latest simulation approaches for modeling them. The topic of “multimaterial PBF techniques” is still very new, undeveloped, and of interest to academia and industry on many levels.


Design/methodology/approach
This is a review paper. The study approach was to carefully search for and investigate notable works and peer-reviewed publications concerning multimaterial three-dimensional printing using PBF techniques. The current methodologies, as well as their advantages and disadvantages, are cross-compared through a systematic review.


Findings
The results show that the development of multimaterial PBF techniques is still in its infancy as many fundamental “research” questions have yet to be addressed before production. Experimentation has many limitations and is costly; therefore, modeling and simulation can be very helpful and is, of course, possible; however, it is heavily dependent on the material data and computational power, so it needs further development in future studies.


Originality/value
This work investigates the multimaterial PBF techniques and discusses the novel printing methods with practical examples. Our literature survey revealed that the number of accounts on the predictive modeling of stresses and optimizing laser scan strategies in multimaterial PBF is low with a (very) limited range of applications. To facilitate future developments in this direction, the key information of the simulation efforts and the state-of-the-art computational models of multimaterial PBF are provided.

## 3. Optimization of selective powder deposition for multi-material powder bed fusion: process innovations and applications
Valid Text Names:

    author = "Girnth, Simon and Wacker, Christian and Waldt, Nils and Klawitter, Günter and Dröder, Klaus",
    title = "Optimization of selective powder deposition for multi-material powder bed fusion: process innovations and applications",

    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issn = "2363-9512"
}


Abstract:
Additive manufacturing processes based on powder bed fusion offer a high degree of design flexibility and enable the processing of a wide range of materials, including metals, ceramics, and polymers, while maintaining minimal porosity. However, production of multi-material components with locally tailored properties to meet specific requirements by incorporating different materials with a high degree of spatial selectivity remains an elusive challenge. Essential prerequisites for achieving this selectivity are specialized selective powder deposition techniques, their development, characterization, and subsequent implementation. In order to investigate optimization potentials and to identify research gaps in the field of selective powder deposition techniques, an evaluation of the current literature is performed in this study, ultimately highlighting promising potentials for vibration-assisted approaches. Key considerations include the reduction of implementation complexity and the downscaling of associated devices to increase their applicability. To achieve implementation simplification, this study derives dimensionless quantities that facilitate a targeted calculation of control parameters by associating powder layer quality metrics with relevant input quantities. The validity of the derived dimensionless quantities is verified by discrete-element method simulations and physical experiments employing a novel miniaturized vibration-assisted device. Metal, ceramic and polymer powders are used as representative samples to demonstrate the versatility of the method for different classes of materials. Ultimately, the presented methods enable a significant improvement in the applicability of vibration-assisted devices and represent an integrative component that provides a suitable basis for further research efforts in the field of combined processing of multiple materials by additive manufacturing technologies that utilize powder beds.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Abstract. Multimaterial additive manufacturing enables the fabrication of components that integrate materials with distinct properties, unlocking unprecedented opportunities for the optimization of functionally integrated mechanical parts. Combined with the design flexibility of additive manufacturing, this capability allows for the creation of innovative solutions tailored to specific performance requirements. A novel platform for multimaterial Powder Bed Fusion – Laser Melting (PBF-LM) has been recently presented with the aim of depositing up to three materials on the same layer to achieve multimaterial products. Despite its unprecedented potential, multimaterial LPBF brings several challenges to achieve high-quality components without flaws or defects. This study leverages real-time process monitoring to aid process parameter optimization for multimaterial LPBF, with a special focus on combining steel and copper alloys for space and energy applications.</jats:p>

## 8. Powder Bed Fusion of Multimaterials

    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/jmmp7010015",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "2504-4494"
}


Abstract:
Powder bed fusion (PBF) process has been used successfully to produce 3D structures using single material properties. The current industrial demand is to use the technology to produce 3D structures of multimaterial properties. An electron beam melting (EBM) process has been used to produce 3D structures of multimaterial properties. However, due to the large beam size, the EBM process lacks geometrical accuracy, requiring a large machining allowance. A laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) process could be used to produce multimaterials with geometrical precision. However, the thermal gradient within the molten pool and the thermophysical difference between the multimaterials leads to defects (weak interfacial bonds, interlayer and intralayer cracks). Other challenges such as poor powder delivery system, powder cross-contamination, and lack of appropriate data processing software for producing 3D multimaterial structures are not yet fully resolved. Nonetheless, there have been encouraging results for producing the next generational multimaterial 3D components of intricate geometrical characteristics.

## pqac-00000023

From text 'neirinck2021powderdepositionsystems pages 5-6'.

Bram Neirinck, Xiaoshuang Li, and Matthias Hick. Powder deposition systems used in powder bed-based multimetal additive manufacturing. ArXiv, 2:387-393, Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1021/accountsmr.1c00030, doi:10.1021/accountsmr.1c00030. This article has 53 citations.

The excerpt describes the working principles and some qualitative capabilities of the Aerosint selective powder deposition recoater. The system uses mechanical forces to hold powder on a drum and release it at chosen locations; each drum deposits one material and can generate a 2D single-material image in a line-by-line manner. Resolution is stated to be directly related to the number and spacing of actuators, while layer thickness is controlled by the applied layer height on the drum and the drum rotation vs. translation speeds. Multi-material capability is achieved by adding dedicated drum and actuator assemblies per material; scaling beyond two materials increases technical complexity and cost. The text notes commercial availability of a dual-material Aerosint recoater and anticipates further development (triple/quad) but does not provide numeric specifications, deposition rate figures, resolution in microns, or pricing. Therefore the excerpt is useful for understanding mechanism, scaling considerations, and the qualitative factors that determine resolution and layer thickness, but lacks the quantitative specs, rates, and price information needed to fully answer the question.

## pqac-00000024

From text 'bareth2025implementationofa pages 1-2'.

Thomas Bareth, Daniel Eder, Timo Schroeder, Maja Lehmann, Georg Schlick, and Christian Seidel. Implementation of a nozzle-based powder deposition mechanism for multi-material powder bed fusion using a laser beam. Production Engineering, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11740-025-01357-8, doi:10.1007/s11740-025-01357-8. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper reports on a robot-guided, nozzle-based powder deposition approach for multi-material PBF-LB. Nozzle methods trade build speed for selective deposition and lower cross-contamination; reported volumetric flow/build rates for nozzle approaches are about 0.4–4.1 mm3 s−1. Demonstrated nozzle/dispensing implementations in the literature include piezo-driven ultrasonic needle dispensers (examples with needle diameters 0.3–0.66 mm giving controllable flow for 316L) and a dual-vibration (ultrasonic + mini-motor) system using a 0.8 mm inner-diameter needle for silicon carbide powder. Such vibration-assisted dry-powder deliveries and fluidization/fluid-assisted methods are commonly used to overcome limited flowability. Resolution (localized deposition width) is governed by nozzle/orifice size and powder track behavior; the study evaluates single- and multi-track depositions and track widths but does not list numeric track-widths in the excerpt. The authors implemented a two-nozzle system integrated into an AMCM M 290-1 machine guided by an ABB IRB1090 robot to selectively deposit copper and ceramic powders (with aluminum applied via the conventional recoater), demonstrating practical multi-material capabilities for three-material parts (e.g., battery cell caps).

## pqac-00000025

From text 'neirinck2021powderdepositionsystems pages 4-5'.

Bram Neirinck, Xiaoshuang Li, and Matthias Hick. Powder deposition systems used in powder bed-based multimetal additive manufacturing. ArXiv, 2:387-393, Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1021/accountsmr.1c00030, doi:10.1021/accountsmr.1c00030. This article has 53 citations.

The excerpt describes nozzle-based (pipette/pipet) powder deposition approaches for multimetal powder-bed fusion. Demonstrated nozzle-based systems include piezo-driven pipet dosing (Manchester University / Chao Wei’s work), vibration-driven pipets, pipet dosing augmented with an integrated vacuum to locally remove powder, and suction-enabled pipet replacements used in a two-step (recoating + local replacement) workflow akin to IGCV. Strengths: pipet dosing is versatile and scalable (adding pipettes enables more materials) and has been used for metal–metal and for glass/metal/ceramic composite coprocessing. Limitations: nozzle diameter required for reliable powder flow constrains the achievable resolution; point-by-point deposition is slow for filling whole beds; suction-depth control can remove more material than desired; vibration-driven pipets can produce low bed density (research into in-process precompaction is ongoing). The excerpt also cites selective-deposition alternatives: Sintef’s Metal Printing Process (MPP) based on electrostatics and Aerosint’s drum-based selective powder deposition (commercialized and integrable with Aconity3D systems). The text does not provide quantitative deposition rates or numeric resolution values.

Review on additive hybrid- and multi-material-manufacturing of metals by powder bed fusion: state of technology and development potential. Progress in Additive Manufacturing, 6:881-894, Aug 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2, doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2. This article has 132 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt categorizes three powder-deposition approaches for 3D multi-material (3D-MM) PBF: holohedral, nozzle-based, and masked. It provides quantitative performance for a suction-based holohedral/multi-chamber system (Anstaett et al.) with an estimated deposition rate of about 60–140 mm3/s versus 240–560 mm3/s for mono-material PBF-LB. Resolution is reported as laser-limited: minimum feature sizes ~130–240 µm plus nominal overlaps of 100–200 µm at 30 µm layer height; parts below 1×1×1 mm3 have been achieved in some alloys. The nozzle-based family is noted to date to the early 1990s and typically relies on interparticular forces with actuators that fluidize powder and deposit it through a nozzle, but this excerpt does not give explicit deposition-rate numbers, detailed resolutions, or a list of demonstrated nozzle-specific systems. The excerpt also describes a multi-material suction/coating workflow requiring at least four coater passes per layer to enable 3D-MM transitions.

Items cited include the Aconity3D Midi+ (flyer), a US patent for a "Coater Apparatus and Method for Additive Manufacturing," Rogren’s "Multi-Materials Three Dimensional Printer" (US patent), Zehavi et al.’s "Selective Powder Delivery for Additive Manufacturing" (WO patent), and a 3D Systems press release about a very large/fast powder-metal 3D printer. The excerpt itself does not give numeric deposition rates, spatial resolution, or detailed descriptions of multi-material capabilities; it only points to specific commercial systems, patent applications, and manufacturer materials that likely contain those quantitative details. To get nozzle-based deposition rates, resolution, and demonstrated multi-material workflows, consult the cited Aconity3D Midi+ flyer, the listed patents (US 2017/0057013 A1; US 9,604,411 B2; WO 2019/074955 A1), the full Neirinck et al. article, and the 3D Systems press release for experimental/demonstrated system performance.

## pqac-00000029

From text 'neirinck2021powderdepositionsystems pages 5-6'.

Bram Neirinck, Xiaoshuang Li, and Matthias Hick. Powder deposition systems used in powder bed-based multimetal additive manufacturing. ArXiv, 2:387-393, Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1021/accountsmr.1c00030, doi:10.1021/accountsmr.1c00030. This article has 53 citations.

The excerpt discusses selective powder-deposition approaches for powder-bed AM rather than explicit nozzle-based ‘‘nozzle’’ systems. It describes drum-based selective deposition (Aerosint) where each drum deposits a single material to produce 2D material images line-by-line; layer thickness is controlled by drum-layer height and drum/translation speeds. Resolution is linked to actuator count and spacing, and repeatability can be ensured after calibration. Aerosint offers a commercial dual-material recoater and is developing a quadruple-deposition unit with Fraunhofer IGCV and Vectoflow, showing scalable multi-material capability by adding dedicated drums/actuators. Pipet-based systems (Iro3D, Grid-Logic) deposit casting sand and metal powder into 3D powder structures but have coarser resolution due to pipette bores “in the mm range.” Sintef explored using a non-sintering support material and concepts for near-net-shape single-material parts. 3D Systems announced a selective powder-deposition approach to limit in-build powder and reported first prints. The excerpt does not provide quantitative nozzle-based deposition rates nor numeric resolution metrics for nozzle systems; it mainly documents demonstrated systems (Aerosint, Sintef, Iro3D, Grid-Logic, 3D Systems) and qualitative notes on resolution and multi-material scaling.

## pqac-00000030

From text 'bandyopadhyay2018additivemanufacturingof pages 7-9'.

Amit Bandyopadhyay and Bryan Heer. Additive manufacturing of multi-material structures. Materials Science and Engineering: R: Reports, 129:1-16, Jul 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mser.2018.04.001, doi:10.1016/j.mser.2018.04.001. This article has 1247 citations.

The excerpt notes that conventional metal powder bed fusion (PBF) processes are generally limited to single-material builds and "simply cannot easily accomplish multi-material processing due to the characteristics of the process." It contrasts PBF with direct deposition routes: powder-based direct deposition is highlighted as better suited for intricate, multi-material metallic structures because reducing feedstock from wire to powder increases packing density and resolution. Quantitative capability given for powder-based direct deposition is "dimensional accuracy at approximately 25–130 μm and a surface roughness of 1–2 μm." The text lists common metal AM processes (DMLM/DMLF, EBM, LENS, DLD/DED/DMD, LMWD, EBF/EBAM) and explains wire-fed processes can readily change feedstock mid-build ("the wire being fed under the beam can be easily changed during the process to make a multi-material part"); compositionally graded parts can be produced by combining different wires or combining wire with metallic powders in a single build. However, the excerpt does not provide nozzle-based powder deposition rates, detailed nozzle resolutions, nor list specific nozzle deposition systems for PBF nozzle-based powder delivery.

## pqac-00000031

From text 'wei2021recentprogressand pages 1-3'.

Chao Wei and Lin Li. Recent progress and scientific challenges in multi-material additive manufacturing via laser-based powder bed fusion. Virtual and Physical Prototyping, 16:347-371, May 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/17452759.2021.1928520, doi:10.1080/17452759.2021.1928520. This article has 320 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes material-spreading approaches used for multi-material laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF). A blade-based dual-powder recoater that uses two powder containers has been demonstrated to produce vertical bimetallic builds (example: copper–stainless steel) but results in sharp material interfaces that can cause high stress concentrations. An improved blade-based recoater with in situ powder mixing was shown (Scaramuccia et al., 2020) to produce a vertical Ti6Al4V/In718 functionally graded material (FGM). However, blade-based spreading as described cannot deposit different powders within the same build layer, limiting some multi-material configurations. The paper also provides typical L-PBF resolution-related process ranges: powder sizes ~10–50 μm (for L-PBF), laser spot diameters ~50–80 μm, and layer thicknesses <100 μm, which indicate the achievable geometrical resolution for L-PBF. The excerpt does not provide nozzle-based powder deposition rates or specific nozzle-system demonstrations; it focuses on blade-based and in situ mixing recoater systems and highlights the remaining challenge of within-layer dissimilar powder deposition.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000032

From text 'jiang2023autonomousbiomimeticsolid pages 8-10'.

Ying Jiang, Hatem Fakhruldeen, Gabriella Pizzuto, Louis Longley, Ai He, Tianwei Dai, Rob Clowes, Nicola Rankin, and Andrew I. Cooper. Autonomous biomimetic solid dispensing using a dual-arm robotic manipulator. Digital Discovery, 2:1733-1744, Jan 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d3dd00075c, doi:10.1039/d3dd00075c. This article has 59 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports performance observations for the Mettler Toledo Quantos and the Chemspeed GDU-family (referred to as Chemspeed (GDU-Access Pfd) / GDU) in a comparative dispensing study. Quantos achieves <2% percentage error on non-challenging solids at a 200 mg target (Table 5 shows Quantos non-challenging solids around −0.41% with a 1.80% value shown), but certain powders are problematic: potassium acetate, molecular sieves, ammonium acetate, and highly abrasive materials such as silicon carbide/sand can cause major issues and even irreparable damage to Quantos dispensing heads. Failures often stem from dispensing-mechanism blockages. Table 4 (sodium nitrite timings) shows Quantos is relatively fast for larger targets (e.g., the table row includes "1000 ... 40   4" and "200 ... 31   3" suggesting ~40 s for 1000 mg and ~31 s for 200 mg in the Quantos column), but performance depends strongly on material, target weight and tolerance. The Chemspeed GDU is described as using a dispensing cartridge / dosing head; it experienced failures with some powders (e.g., poor performance with sugar) and mechanical failures related to closing/opening of the dispensing cartridge. Chemspeed timing entries in Table 4 are e.g. "1000 ... 134   17", "200 ... 109   19", "20 ... 62   23" indicating different throughput characteristics by target mass. Overall, the excerpt gives accuracy (percent error) and timing/throughput data and lists powder types that work well versus those that are challenging for each platform.

From text 'german20183pc036smallscalecompounding pages 1-1'.

O. German and M. Paulsson. 3pc-036 small-scale compounding using a powder dispensing tool for individualised solid dosage form drug delivery. Section 3: Production and compounding, 25:A40-A40, Mar 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1136/ejhpharm-2018-eahpconf.88, doi:10.1136/ejhpharm-2018-eahpconf.88. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes use of a Mettler Toledo Quantos handheld powder dosing system (model HPD) with an orifice size of 2.5/4 mm, used alongside a Mettler Toledo microbalance MT5 in a master’s project to fill single capsules. The authors report that the HPD Quantos "can be part of a method for preparing individual solid dosage forms, the equivalent of one-fifth of a tablet." They also state "The handheld version of Quantos is not accurate enough for small capsule sizes." Materials tested included crushed Allopurinol Teva 100 mg tablets and Cellets microcrystalline cellulose pellets; various Coni-snap capsule sizes were screened. The excerpt does not provide a numerical accuracy in milligrams, a detailed dispensing mechanism beyond the device name and orifice size, nor any specification or throughput data for a Chemspeed GDU-P—those details are not present in the provided text.

Matthew N. Bahr, David B. Damon, Simon D. Yates, Alexander S. Chin, J. David Christopher, Samuel Cromer, Nicholas Perrotto, Jorge Quiroz, and Victor Rosso. Collaborative evaluation of commercially available automated powder dispensing platforms for high-throughput experimentation in pharmaceutical applications. Organic Process Research & Development, 22:1500-1508, Oct 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.oprd.8b00259, doi:10.1021/acs.oprd.8b00259. This article has 63 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Relevant details in the excerpt about the Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5: its dosing head uses an embedded RFID chip and a learning/adaptive algorithm during initial dispenses to optimize accuracy and speed, and the dispense head employs a combination of rotary and tapping actions. The study evaluated target masses of 2, 10, and 50 mg and reports that % error improved as target mass increased. The algorithm learned dosing parameters quickly for powders such as D-mannitol, L-proline, PVPP and thiamine HCl, while fumed silica, limestone, and sodium chloride required several dispenses for optimization. The excerpt does not provide explicit accuracy values in milligrams or absolute mg error figures. About Chemspeed: the text describes Chemspeed SWING systems having an optional “Teaching” operation that performs initial dispenses to set parameters, that all SWING dispenses were performed in a purge box environment, and that fumed silica could not be dispensed at the 50 mg target due to very low bulk density. The excerpt does not contain specific Chemspeed GDU-P specifications or throughput numbers.

This article has 63 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies the Mettler-Toledo Quantos implementation as the Quantos QB5 system equipped with the QH012-LNMP dispense head and a carousel layout. It notes the Quantos QB5 is limited to dispensing one-to-30 powders (i.e., not high-density plate-based many-to-many dispensing) and showed a balanced combination of low % error, low % RSD mass, and low dispense time across the seven powders tested. Reported dispense times for the Quantos systems begin at about 10 seconds (range line is truncated in the excerpt). For Chemspeed, the text discusses SWING/SDU platforms (not a GDU-P in this excerpt): Chemspeed SWING/SDU had the shortest dispense times of the platforms tested, with Chemspeed SWING dispense times reported from 13 to 65 s. The SDU technology is described as well-suited for dense, large-size, fast-flowing powders such as sodium chloride, but Chemspeed systems experienced a relatively large percentage of machine stalls that required researcher intervention. Chemspeed and Unchained platforms offer many-to-many and 96-well plate dispensing integrated with liquid handling. The excerpt does not mention a Chemspeed GDU-P model or provide explicit mg-level accuracy numbers for Quantos or Chemspeed.

From text 'bahr2018collaborativeevaluationof pages 5-7'.

Matthew N. Bahr, David B. Damon, Simon D. Yates, Alexander S. Chin, J. David Christopher, Samuel Cromer, Nicholas Perrotto, Jorge Quiroz, and Victor Rosso. Collaborative evaluation of commercially available automated powder dispensing platforms for high-throughput experimentation in pharmaceutical applications. This article has 63 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports that the Mettler-Toledo Quantos (QB5 and QH012-LNMP heads) showed general utility across the powder/target-mass combinations tested; its dispense head employs a combination of rotary and tapping actions, which likely contributes to its performance. The study indicates which powders were easier or harder to dispense: L-proline and thiamine HCl (less dense/smaller organics) dispensed better overall, PVPP (polymeric) performed well across platforms, while fumed silica, limestone powder, and sodium chloride (inorganics) were most challenging. Low-target dispenses are less reliable: 2 mg targets had substantially higher %RSDs (190%–680% higher than 10 mg). For Chemspeed, the SWING/SDU systems had the shortest dispense times; SDU technology was particularly suited to dense, large, fast-flowing powders such as sodium chloride, and smaller-thread dispense heads (type 1/type 1D) improved control for better-flowing powders. The excerpt does not provide explicit accuracy figures in milligrams for the Quantos heads, nor does it include specifications or throughput numbers for a Chemspeed GDU-P unit.

function.arguments: {"question": "What is the HT-READ (high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development) approach and how does it handle powder mixing and dispensing for combinatorial alloy libraries?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

function.arguments: {"question": "What open-source or low-cost automated powder dispensing solutions exist for research laboratories? What are prices for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000038

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 7-11'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The HT-READ approach is an integrated high-throughput alloy development pipeline combining additive manufacturing (DED), computational screening (e.g., CALPHAD), a 16-sample ring library, and automated characterization to rapidly fabricate and test many alloy compositions. For combinatorial powder handling the demonstrated workflow uses pre-mixed powders: alloy powders (In625 and alloying elements) and a 200 ppm alumina flow aid are weighed, blended on a rolling mill, and loaded into designated vials in a Formalloy Alloy Development Feeder (ADF). The ADF contains 16 separate vials that rotate under computer control so one vial feeds the DED nozzle at a time, enabling up to 16 distinct compositions per build; vials are re-filled with new pre-mixed powders for each subsequent 16-ring sample. The paper notes alternative in-situ powder-mixing strategies that meter elemental powder feeds from repositories, but cautions these methods face significant hurdles when expanding beyond 3–4 elements. Consequently the authors suggest automating powder weighing, mixing, and vial filling to maintain composition fidelity for higher-component combinatorial libraries.


---

## pqac-00000039

From text 'moorehead2020highthroughputsynthesisof pages 1-5'.

Michael Moorehead, Kaila Bertsch, Michael Niezgoda, Calvin Parkin, Mohamed Elbakhshwan, Kumar Sridharan, Chuan Zhang, Dan Thoma, and Adrien Couet. High-throughput synthesis of mo-nb-ta-w high-entropy alloys via additive manufacturing. Materials & Design, 187:108358, Feb 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2019.108358, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2019.108358. This article has 310 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The paper describes a high-throughput, in situ alloying approach using directed energy deposition (DED) on a LENS MR-7 to produce discrete combinatorial libraries of bulk (3D) alloy samples on a single build plate. Composition variation is achieved by using four independently controlled powder hoppers whose powders are fed by augers into a common argon gas line. Powders are aggregated in the central gas line and mixed by turbulent gas flow during transit to the printhead; the mixed powder is then sprayed through four nozzles into a laser-induced melt pool where alloying occurs. This setup enables printing of different HEA compositions as arrays (not mere thin gradients), facilitating subsequent high-throughput SEM/EDS/XRD characterization while samples remain on the build plate. The described powder feeding and mixing strategy (independent hopper control + central mixing in gas flow) is the mechanism by which combinatorial compositions are dispensed and varied across the sample array.


---

## pqac-00000040

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 1-4'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the HT-READ approach as an integrated, closed-loop high-throughput alloy development workflow that unifies computational screening (e.g., CALPHAD and machine learning), automated synthesis, processing, characterization, testing, and data analysis. An AI agent links compositions to properties and new experimental data can feed subsequent iterations. To enable automation and high-throughput characterization (XRD, SEM/EDS, EBSD), HT-READ uses miniaturized sample “libraries” rather than one-sample-at-a-time testing: a 32 mm metallurgical mount containing a rotationally symmetric ring of 16 discrete samples. The library is fabricated by 3D printing and each sample is uniquely identified and stored to maintain persistent sample-data linkage. The rotational geometry is chosen to simplify automation (favoring sample rotation over X-Y-Z translation) and to meet geometric constraints of electron- and x-ray-based techniques. The authors demonstrate the workflow using alloyed Inconel 625 as a model system. The provided excerpt does not describe any procedures or hardware for powder mixing or powder dispensing for combinatorial library fabrication.

messages.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000041

From text 'hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 4-5'.

Miguel Hernández-del-Valle, Jorge Ilarraza-Zuazo, Enrique Dios-Lázaro, Javier Rubio, Joris Audoux, and Maciej Haranczyk. Pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor: open hardware for nanocomposite space exploration via automated material compounding. Digital Discovery, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d4dd00198b, doi:10.1039/d4dd00198b. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes two open-hardware, low-cost automated pellet/powder handling devices intended for lab-scale material compounding: the Pellet dispensomixer (dispenser) and a Pellet distributor (robotized tube arm). Both are Arduino-driven (UNO or MEGA as required) using stepper motors and A4988 drivers; control is via serial communication with a computer and the projects include Arduino code and Python notebooks (inverse kinematics via ikpy) in a public GitHub repository. All designs and CAD/STL files are provided for 3D printing, and the ESI includes a detailed Bill of Materials. Cost estimates given: the dispensomixer ~250 euros (~270 USD), an optional air ionizer used to mitigate static charges adds roughly 571 euros (~600 USD), the pellet distributor is quoted at ~150 euros (~160 USD) to build, and an electronic balance used in integration is ~285 euros (~300 USD). The device routines include motor-direction alternation to reduce blocking, and the systems are designed to integrate feedback from balances to control dispensing and container switching.


---

## pqac-00000042

From text 'hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 5-6'.

Miguel Hernández-del-Valle, Jorge Ilarraza-Zuazo, Enrique Dios-Lázaro, Javier Rubio, Joris Audoux, and Maciej Haranczyk. Pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor: open hardware for nanocomposite space exploration via automated material compounding. Digital Discovery, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d4dd00198b, doi:10.1039/d4dd00198b. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This excerpt describes an open-hardware “pellet dispensomixer/pellet distributor” intended for lab-scale automated compounding of polymer pellets and masterbatches. The authors report the device as low-cost: the estimated bill-of-materials cost is around 150 euros (~160 USD). The project provides code and examples via GitHub repositories for operation and customization. Performance metrics from a PLA + masterbatch test include a measured dispensing accuracy of 99.33% and a mass flow of 7.59 g min−1 (≈454.15 g h−1). The design uses interchangeable rotors to change pellet shape and flow; the upper pellet size is limited by rotor-hole diameter. The control strategy dispenses small iterative increments (e.g., 1 g steps for the least-abundant component) with balance feedback and corrections to avoid cumulative error. Critically, the device is explicitly not designed for powders: powders exhibit different aggregation mechanics and can obstruct moving parts. The paper notes that CAD modifications could adapt geometry, but warns about powder handling limitations. Safety advice (e.g., proper wiring and common ground for the Arduino/12 V supply) and repository resources are provided.


---

## pqac-00000043

From text 'hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 1-2'.

Miguel Hernández-del-Valle, Jorge Ilarraza-Zuazo, Enrique Dios-Lázaro, Javier Rubio, Joris Audoux, and Maciej Haranczyk. Pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor: open hardware for nanocomposite space exploration via automated material compounding. Digital Discovery, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d4dd00198b, doi:10.1039/d4dd00198b. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes an open-hardware approach for automating polymer compounding focused on two devices: a multi-material pellet dispensomixer and a pellet collector–distributor. The authors position this work as “open laboratory hardware” to automate material input to extruders and facilitate changing compositions during loading. It notes that dispensing solids is especially challenging due to particle size, shape and electrostatics, and cites prior work (M. N. Bahr) on high-throughput automated powder dispensing. The excerpt contrasts expensive commercial lab-scale powder dispensers (examples given: FLEX Powderdose, Chemspeed SWING GDU-Pfd, Mettler Toledo CHRONECT Quantos) with DIY/open solutions built on common microcontrollers and SBCs — “computers such as Arduino, Raspberry Pi and others” — and mentions adapted pipettes/liquid handlers as low-cost precedents. The paper’s devices aim to simplify preparation of targeted compositions from master batches and to integrate into automated workflows. The excerpt does not contain any pricing or specific cost figures for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing solutions.


---

## pqac-00000044

From text 'hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 2-3'.

Miguel Hernández-del-Valle, Jorge Ilarraza-Zuazo, Enrique Dios-Lázaro, Javier Rubio, Joris Audoux, and Maciej Haranczyk. Pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor: open hardware for nanocomposite space exploration via automated material compounding. Digital Discovery, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d4dd00198b, doi:10.1039/d4dd00198b. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes both commercial and open/DIY powder/pellet dispensing solutions. Commercial laboratory systems mentioned include FLEX Powderdose, Chemspeed SWING GDU-Pfd and Mettler Toledo CHRONECT Quantos. The authors situate their work within a growing open-source and DIY laboratory equipment movement, noting DIY setups can offer "enhanced customization at prices that can be around 94% less than their commercial analogues." Examples of alternative approaches include a system by Y. Jiang et al. that handles both powder and pellets, biomimetic robot-arm approaches (spatula + balance), and designs based on rotors or auger mechanisms. The Pellet dispensomixer presented is specifically designed for pellet-form materials, inspired by the Open Source Food Pellet Dispenser, and is paired with a novel pellet distributor for laboratory compounding (claimed to have no predecessor in the field). Design choices favored rotor-with-holes solutions, with iterative features (semicircular holes, ramps, fillets, rotor shaking) to prevent clogging. Characterization data in the excerpt give an average pellet mass of 0.009184 g (SD 0.003366 g) and an estimated sampling error of about 0.29% for a 150 g sample. The excerpt does not list an explicit purchase/build price for the Pellet dispensomixer itself.


---

## pqac-00000045

From text 'jiang2023autonomousbiomimeticsolid pages 1-2'.

Ying Jiang, Hatem Fakhruldeen, Gabriella Pizzuto, Louis Longley, Ai He, Tianwei Dai, Rob Clowes, Nicola Rankin, and Andrew I. Cooper. Autonomous biomimetic solid dispensing using a dual-arm robotic manipulator. Digital Discovery, 2:1733-1744, Jan 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d3dd00075c, doi:10.1039/d3dd00075c. This article has 59 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This excerpt describes an automated solid-dispensing platform based on a highly dexterous dual-arm robotic manipulator that mimics a scientist using a spatula and analytical balance. The system uses a fuzzy-logic controller to choose motion parameters and manipulate various spatula sizes, transferring milligram to gram quantities. It reportedly handles a broad range of materials, including those that abrade or block moving-part dispensers, and can reset and re-dispense if weight exceeds a tolerance. The platform achieved dispensing accuracy as low as 2 mg and is described as significantly cheaper than most commercial solid-dispensing platforms. The excerpt also summarizes commercial options (Chemspeed GDU-S SWILE, GDU-Pfd, Quantos, Chronect XPR) and their mechanisms—gravity plus mechanical agitation, piston plus glass capillary with internal balance, crescent valve, and spiral metal impellers. The text does not provide price points, open-source project names, or mention the Pellet dispensomixer or DIY powder-dosing systems, so additional sources are needed for cost figures and open-source/DIY options.

      "rationale": "Found K-Tron KT-20 data, Brabender, Schenck AccuRate, Gericke GCM-250 references from pharma continuous manufacturing literature",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "K-Tron KT-20 mass flow range 32-3540 g/h, motor drive 20-90%. Brabender twin-screw gravimetric feeders, Schenck AccuRate AP-300 and DP4, Gericke GCM-250 blenders all referenced in pharmaceutical continuous manufacturing lines. Specific accuracy numbers (e.g., RSD) not provided in literature for some but general ±1% RSD in gravimetric mode is industry standard.",
      "evaluation": "Adequate for landscape review. Supplemented with Rao 2021 which lists Brabender, Coperion, Schenck AccuRate as major suppliers."
    },

Multiple research groups have bespoke solutions. Resolution governed by actuator spacing/nozzle diameter. Commercial dual-material Aerosint recoater available.",

DIY solutions (e.g., LEGO-based at ~350 EUR) exist but for liquids only.",

      "status": "completed",

Used extensively for AM powder characterization (316L, Ti-6Al-4V, etc.). Shear cell area 1879 mm2, column height 44.5mm. Provides BFE, SE, ffc indices. Not a dispensing system per se, but critical for powder qualification.",
      "evaluation": "Good characterization data. FT4 is primarily a characterization tool, not a dispensing tool."
    },
    {
      "id": 6,
      "objective": "Gather additional evidence on combinatorial alloy discovery methods and DIY/open-source powder dosing",
      "rationale": "Found HT-READ workflow with Formalloy ADF, LENS MR-7 with 4 hoppers, and open-hardware pellet dispensomixer",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "HT-READ uses pre-mixed powders loaded into Formalloy ADF (16 vials). LENS MR-7 uses 4 independent auger-fed hoppers with central gas mixing. Open-hardware pellet dispensomixer costs ~250 EUR, pellet distributor ~150 EUR. Pellet dispensomixer not designed for powders. DIY syringe pump at ~350 EUR. Commercial systems are expensive and powder-specific.",
      "evaluation": "Good additional data for gap analysis section."
    },
    {
      "id": 7,
      "objective": "Create summary artifacts (tables) comparing systems and submit final answer",
      "rationale": "User expects a comprehensive landscape review with specific details organized clearly",
      "status": "pending",

      "evaluation": null
    }
