snippets: Figure 5. Mass error vs dispense time for all platforms.

Dispense time data and mass dispensed data from each automated powder dispensing platform were aggregated by powder, target mass, and dispense head, and then quantiﬁed by calculating mean and % RSD mass values for each subset.
Dispense times increased as the target mass increased from 2 to 10 to 50 mg
Of the 2776 dispenses on the Chemspeed SWING systems, 21.4% had dispense time data.
Of the 3692 dispenses on the Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5 systems, dispense time data were only recorded from two of the three installed systems.
Each system was capable of dispensing powders with comparable mean mass values.

Dispenses with a 2 mg target mass had on average 190% to 680% higher % RSD mass values than dispenses with a 10 mg target mass, and 260% to 1700% higher % RSD mass values than dispenses with a 50 mg target mass.
The Chemspeed SWING/SDU systems generally had the shortest dispense times
Optimal dispense head selection was essential for best performance for both the Chemspeed SWING and Unchained Laboratories systems.
This reveals a gap in the ability for current systems to accurately dispense low-target masses.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a systematic study comparing four off‑the‑shelf automated powder dispensers (including Chemspeed SWING, Mettler‑Toledo Quantos QB5, and Unchained Laboratories heads/systems). It notes that each platform uses its own dispense heads and that Chemspeed and Unchained classic heads used rotary action, Unchained SV used tapping, and Quantos used both. The study collected mass dispensed (mg) and dispense time (sec) per dispense; dispense time excludes motion/setup time. Accuracy was evaluated as % error = (mass dispensed − target mass)/target mass ×100%. Target masses were 2, 10, and 50 mg (fumed silica only at 2 and 10 mg because 50 mg would not fit in the 1 mL vials). Table 3 reports numbers and % of dispenses retained for analysis by system and powder, with overall retained dispenses of 2776 (67.1%) for Chemspeed, 3692 (99.8%) for Mettler‑Toledo Quantos, 3750 (94.7%) for one Unchained system and 5957 (99.3%) for another, totaling 16175 (90.9%). The excerpt does not provide specific numeric accuracy/precision (mean % error or %RSD) summaries, throughput specs (e.g., dispenses/hour) or any pricing information.

snippets: Four commercially available, oﬀ-the-shelf, automated powder dispensing platforms from three manufacturers were selected for this study (Table 1).
Each platform used its own dispense heads to contain the powder and dispense it into vials.

The Chemspeed SWING SDU and Unchained Laboratories classic dispense head technologies used rotary action, the Unchained Laboratories SV head technology used tapping action, and the Mettler-Toledo Quantos technology used both rotary and tapping actions.
The mass dispensed (mg) and dispense time (sec) data were obtained from the automated powder dispensing systems.

The dispense time refers to the time required for a given powder dispense to a well to complete, and it does not include the time required to move the vial, plate, or dispense head into position prior to the dispense.
% error of each mass dispensed was deﬁned as the diﬀerence between the mass dispensed and the target mass, divided by the target mass, and multiplied by 100% (see eq 1).
Target mass: 2, 10, and 50 mg.
Vials: 8 × 30 mm 1 mL shell vials.
Attempts to dispense fumed silica on the Chemspeed SWING and Unchained Labs Freeslate systems were unsuccessful.
Fumed silica was only tested at 2 and 10 mg since 50 mg would not ﬁt in a 1 mL vial.

contexts.context: The excerpt provides measured accuracy, precision and throughput comparisons for Mettler Toledo Quantos, a Chemspeed GDU-style system, and a dual‑arm robotic dispensing prototype. Throughput (dispensing time for sodium nitrite with 2 mg tolerance) from Table 4: at 1000 mg: Dual‑arm 552 ±128 s, Quantos 40 ±4 s, Chemspeed 134 ±17 s; at 500 mg: Dual‑arm 517 ±72 s, Quantos 33 ±2 s, Chemspeed 132 ±21 s; at 200 mg: Dual‑arm 381 ±73 s, Quantos 31 ±3 s, Chemspeed 109 ±19 s; at 20 mg: Dual‑arm 239 ±30 s, Quantos 285 ±2 s, Chemspeed 62 ±23 s. Accuracy/precision at 200 mg (Table 5): for non‑challenging solids Quantos ≈ −0.41% (±1.80%), dual‑arm ≈ 0.07% (±0.56%), Chemspeed ≈ −2.23% (±12.39%). The paper notes a trade‑off between dispensing time and precision and documents failure modes (mechanical blockages, deliquescence, robot errors) and which solids are challenging for each platform. The excerpt does not provide purchase prices, nor any data about the Unchained Labs system, so pricing and Unchained Labs specifications are absent.

snippets: The dispensing time was also measured for sodium nitrite for all three platforms under different target values with 2 mg tolerance and the results are listed in Table 4.
Compared with the two commercial platforms, the dispensing time on our dual-arm robot platform was significantly longer, particularly when compared to the Quantos system.
As such, there was a trade-off between the dispensing time and the target precision.
Table 4  Comparison of the dispensing time (seconds) for sodium
For non-challenging solids, the percentage error was less than 2% for the Quantos system and the dual-arm robot.
The average error for these solids was just 0.07% for the dual-arm robot at this 200 mg scale.
For the Chemspeed platform, the most challenging solids were molecular sieves, ammonium acetate, potassium acetate, lithium hydroxide monohydrate, and aluminium oxide.
For the Quantos platform, the challenging solids included potassium acetate, molecular sieves and ammonium acetate.

For the dual-arm robot platform, the challenging solids were calcium carbonate, potassium acetate, and ammonium acetate due to the compressibility (CaCO3) or hygroscopic properties (NH4CH3CO2 and CH3COOK) that can cause solids to stick on the inside of funnels and spatulas; in some cases, this caused the funnel to be blocked.
Failure in the dispensing process was defined as the non-completion of a single dispensing procedure.

contexts.context: This excerpt from Bahr et al. (2018) describes an experimental comparison of automated powder dispensers (Chemspeed SWING, Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5, Unchained Laboratories Freeslate/Junior). Test conditions included target masses of 2, 10, and 50 mg (fumed silica only at 2 and 10 mg because 50 mg would not fit in 1 mL vials), with 1 run = 20 dispenses and multiple runs per configuration (120 dispenses per configuration for fumed silica, 180 for others). In total 17,797 dispenses were attempted and 16,175 retained for analysis after removing 1,622 discounted dispenses (machine stalls, timeouts, questionable values, zero values with counts given). The study reports that % error improved as target mass increased from 2 → 10 → 50 mg and shows % error distributions in figures by powder, target mass, and dispense head. Platform-level features noted: Chemspeed has a “Teaching” mode to set parameters, Mettler-Toledo Quantos dosing heads include an embedded RFID learning chip, and Unchained Labs systems use an adaptive learning algorithm. The excerpt documents environment and dispense-head effects and some powders (e.g., fumed silica) that could not be dispensed on certain platforms. The excerpt does not provide explicit numerical accuracy/precision specifications, throughput rates (dispenses/hour), or pricing; such data are not present here.

snippets: Target mass: 2, 10, and 50 mg.
Fumed silica was only tested at 2 and 10 mg since 50 mg would not ﬁt in a 1 mL vial.
Attempts to dispense fumed silica on the Chemspeed SWING and Unchained Labs Freeslate systems were unsuccessful.
Number of dispenses: 1 run = 20 dispenses of a powder at a given target mass into 20 separate vials.
for a total of 6 runs for fumed silica and 9 runs for all other powders.f
A total of 17,797 dispenses were made in the study.
From this, 1622 dispenses were discounted from the analysis, and 16 175 dispenses were retained for analysis.
Discounted dispenses fell into one of four categories:
Machine stall: a run which aborted prior to its desired conclusion due to lack of powder ﬂow during a given dispense (103 dispenses = 0.6%)
Machine time out: a dispense which ended due to a time out error caused by low powder ﬂow, but the run continued with the subsequent dispense (153 dispenses = 0.9%)
Questionable value: a dispense with an outlier amount of mass dispensed (39 dispenses = 0.2%)
Zero value: an intended dispense in which no powder was dispensed to a single vial in a run (67 dispenses = 0.4%)
As the target mass values increased from 2 to 10 to 50 mg, the % error values improved for all powders.
Chemspeed platform has an optional operation called “Teaching” which performs several initial dispenses to set dispensing parameters for all subsequent dispenses.
The Mettler-Toledo dosing head contains an embedded RFID chip which utilizes a learning algorithm during the initial dispenses of a run to optimize accuracy and speed.
Unchained Laboratories powder dispensing platforms include a user software package that contains an adaptive learning algorithm to dispense and weigh powders.q
All dispenses on the three Chemspeed SWING systems were performed in an equivalent purge box environment.
All dispenses on the Unchained Laboratories Junior system were performed in an LEV environment.

contexts.context: This study evaluated commercial powder-dispensing platforms (Chemspeed SWING/SDU, Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5 with QH012-LNMP, and Unchained Laboratories systems) across seven powders and 5,957 dispenses. Key performance findings: overall % error spanned −10% to +3,245% (95.3% between −25% and +100%), with 278 dispenses >+100% error, indicating wide accuracy variation. Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5 (QH012-LNMP) showed a balanced combination of low % error, low % RSD mass, and relatively low dispense times, but its carousel layout limits it to ~1–30 powder dispensing. Chemspeed SWING/SDU had the shortest dispense times (13–65 s) but suffered a large percentage of machine stalls that aborted runs. Unchained Laboratories delivered balanced low % error and %RSD and provided integrated software for data capture and transfer, but performance was sensitive to dispense head choice and equipment environment. The authors note limitations for dispensing target masses <10 mg and highlight data-capture/integration needs. The excerpt does not include price or detailed spec sheets; supporting information reportedly contains full tables of mean, median, %RSD, and dispense times for retained dispenses.
contexts.question: What are the accuracy, precision, throughput specifications and prices of commercial automated powder dispensing systems like Mettler Toledo Quantos, Chemspeed, and Unchained Labs for laboratory use?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports experimental characterization of a Coperion K-Tron KT-20 C-gearbox loss-in-weight feeder: 14 materials, four screw types (coarse auger, fine auger, coarse concave, fine concave) and six feed rates were run for a total of 336 experiments. Key findings: motor-driven screw speed (controlled by feedback) sets mass feed rate and an effective motor operating range was observed between 20% and 90% of drive command; the authors define a "feed factor" as the maximum mass flow for a material/feeder/screw at 100% drive. A correlation was found between conditioned bulk density (material packing) and the feed factor, enabling prediction of maximum flow per screw type from powder characterization. The text also references methods evaluating a K-Tron KT-35. The excerpt describes control principles and predictive relationships but does not provide manufacturer specification sheets, explicit numeric feed-rate ranges (e.g., g/min), or stated accuracy/precision percentages for Coperion K-Tron feeders; those specific specs are not present in these pages.
contexts.question: What are the specifications, accuracy, and feed rate capabilities of Coperion K-Tron loss-in-weight feeders for pharmaceutical powder dosing?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports experimental evaluation of a K-Tron KT-20 loss-in-weight (LIW) feeder for pharmaceutical powders using four screw geometries (coarse auger, fine auger, coarse concave, fine concave) and six feed rates. Key findings: (1) feeder throughput capacity shows a linear correlation with conditioned bulk density for each screw type, allowing prediction of a material's maximum throughput (the "feedfactor") when screws run at 100% drive; (2) an optimal motor/drive command operating window with minimal feed variability (RSD) was identified, generally between 20–90% screw speed for three screw types and 40–90% for the fine auger; (3) LIW control relies on monitoring mass change to compute instantaneous feed rate and adjusting motor speed via a feedback loop to match setpoint. These results define a practical design space for KT-20 feeders linking powder characterization (conditioned bulk density) to maximum feed rates and identifying stable screw speed ranges. The excerpt does not provide absolute numeric feed-rate capacities (e.g., g/min ranges) or quantified accuracy/error specifications for Coperion K-Tron feeders beyond these relationships and operating ranges.
contexts.question: What are the specifications, accuracy, and feed rate capabilities of Coperion K-Tron loss-in-weight feeders for pharmaceutical powder dosing?

contexts.context: The provided excerpt explains general operating principles of loss-in-weight (LIW) feeders and highlights differences between gravimetric and volumetric modes relevant to pharmaceutical powder dosing, but it does not list model-specific specifications, numeric accuracies, or feed-rate ranges for Coperion K-Tron feeders. LIW feeders comprise a feeding device on a weighing platform with a control system. In gravimetric mode the system continuously measures mass over time and adjusts the dispensing rate (e.g., screw speed) to minimize deviation from the setpoint, thereby compensating for variations in bulk density—this mode is preferred in pharmaceutical applications for accuracy. Volumetric mode displaces a constant volume per time with a constant screw speed, so mass feed rate is not controlled. The excerpt cites Coperion K-Tron (2012) regarding the control behavior but gives no quantitative performance data (e.g., accuracy in %RSD or RE, minimum/maximum feed-rate limits). Therefore the excerpt provides useful conceptual context about how Coperion K-Tron LIW control works but lacks the specific numeric specifications and performance metrics requested.
contexts.question: What are the specifications, accuracy, and feed rate capabilities of Coperion K-Tron loss-in-weight feeders for pharmaceutical powder dosing?

snippets: Loss-in-weight (LIW) feeders consist of a feeding device, weighing platform and control system (Engisch and Muzzio, 2015a).
In gravimetric mode, the control system acquires the mass of the feeding device and its content from the weighing platform as a function of time during feeding.
The control system minimizes the difference between the actual feed rate and the feed rate setpoint by adjusting the dispensing rate (e.g., screw speed) of the feeding device (Coperion K-Tron, 2012).
The volumetric mode is characterized by the displacement of a constant material volume per unit of time instead of a constant mass per unit of time as in gravimetric mode.
The screw speed of the feeding device is kept constant during volumetric feeding, implying that the powder feed rate is not controlled (Blackshields and Crean, 2017).
For pharmaceutical applications, where the feeding accuracy of the individual raw materials is critical and the density of the processed powders can vary, the gravimetric feeding mode is preferred.

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several challenges relevant to powder dispensing and high-throughput multi-material AM alloy discovery. High-throughput approaches rely on in situ alloying and element diffusion under the laser, and gradient powder spreading has been used to produce hundreds of samples quickly while smoothing sharp compositional gradients and achieving compositional control precision better than 0.1 wt.%/mm. Key gaps include handling materials with different melting points and laser absorption rates — the excerpt notes that "the relationship between compositional changes and laser settings could be an important area for future research." Conventional analytical workflows (e.g., sectioning) are described as inefficient for the large parameter arrays produced, creating a bottleneck and driving the need for new high-throughput analysis techniques. The authors also flag limitations in computational models (equilibrium-based simulations) versus non-equilibrium experimental results and recommend shared experimental databases to improve predictive accuracy. The excerpt does not describe specific automated powder mixing, micro-dosing, or feeder hardware solutions; it highlights gradient powder spreading and the need for integrated laser-parameter mapping, analytics, and databases rather than listing commercial or bespoke automated dispensing systems.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt evaluates automated powder dispensers and highlights factors and gaps that affect dispensing performance. Powder properties (density, particle size/size distribution, flowability) and target mass strongly influence accuracy and dispense time: low-target masses (e.g., 2 mg) show dramatically higher %RSD than 10 or 50 mg, revealing a clear gap in accurately dispensing low masses. Certain powders—notably inorganic powders such as fumed silica, limestone powder, and sodium chloride—were most challenging to dispense. Hardware choices matter: dispense-head selection substantially affects results, and different platforms performed differently (e.g., Chemspeed SWING/SDU systems generally had the shortest dispense times and were well-suited for dense, large, fast-flowing powders). The equipment environment (glovebox vs open bench) impacted performance for some systems. Additional operational gaps include machine stalls and limited integration: the authors note a need for seamless data transfer and integration across HTE ecosystems. The excerpt provides general lessons on powder dosing challenges and platform-specific strengths/gaps but does not describe metal-specific AM mixing or dosing solutions.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights powder-dispensing and compositional-control aspects of a LDED-based high-throughput workflow. It notes that compositional ratios are "controlled by mixer speed" and that the approach enables "interlayer powder change," with a reported "compositional change ratio per layer >5 wt.%." Gradient powder spreading is described as a way to "significantly smooth sharp compositional gradients," improving precision, and the method enabled "the simultaneous preparation of over 200 tensile samples." Challenges and gaps called out include difficulty to "control composition distribution," limits on per-layer composition adjustment, and process issues tied to high laser energy (deep melt pools) and immiscible-alloy cracking. The text stresses that "different alloys have varying melting points and  laser  absorption  rates," and recommends that "establishing a laser-parameter database for different metals and alloys is crucial," because "Laser parameters...require trials for new alloy compositions, making the process cumbersome." The excerpt implies some automated/controlled dispensing features (mixer-speed control, interlayer powder change, and gradient powder spreading) but does not detail broader automated powder-mixing or dosing systems.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several gaps and limitations in powder dispensing for multi-material PBF relevant to high-throughput alloy discovery. Reported nozzle-based feed rates translate to roughly 0.4–4.1 mm3/s per nozzle (examples: 5–15 mm/s travel giving 10–30 mg/s; up to 32 mg/s for 316L), and powder/part feature sizes range from ~85 µm powder tracks up to millimeter-scale deposited lines (0.8–1.6 mm). Nozzle-based concepts are at MRL 4–5, while masked/photoconductor approaches are earlier (MRL ~2) and lack published deposition rates. Pressure-based masked systems (suction drums with valve matrices) can deposit longer powder lines quickly (demo: 150 mm in ~8 s), suggesting higher throughput but feature-resolution limitations. Key challenges include limited deposition rate data for some methods, relatively coarse minimum feature sizes for deposited powder lines, early technology maturity, and integration gaps: material distribution/design tools, local material-property definition, software limits, process monitoring, and understanding of metallurgical mixing/heat-affected zones during in-situ alloying. The excerpt documents existing deposition strategies—nozzle arrays, pressure-based drums, photoconductor masking and suction/coater combinations—but does not detail automated lab-scale powder mixing/dosing hardware solutions beyond these process concepts.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes automation needs for polymer compounding and introduces open hardware (a multi-material pellet dispenser and a pellet collector-distributor) to automate dispensing and distribution of solid thermoplastic materials. It highlights that compounding workflows remain labor intensive (dispensing, weighing, mixing, extruding) and that dispensing solids is especially challenging due to variable particle sizes, shapes and electrostatic interactions. Prior high-throughput powder-dispensing studies (Bahr et al.) largely use gravity-based approaches where powder is held or released by control systems; commercial examples cited include FLEX Powderdose, Chemspeed SWING GDU-Pfd and Mettler Toledo CHRONECT Quantos. The authors note there are both commercial and DIY options, but that commercial lab-scale solutions are expensive and have limitations. The excerpt focuses on thermoplastic pellets/powders and on general powder-dispensing challenges; it does not specifically address metal additive-manufacturing powders or metal alloy dosing/mixing requirements, indicating a gap in metal-AM–specific automated powder dosing solutions within this text.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses powder-deposition approaches and limitations for multi-material L-PBF. Common implementations use dual powder containers and a blade recoater to switch powders between layers, enabling vertical bimetallic builds but producing sharp, abrupt interfaces that concentrate stress and risk delamination. An improved blade-based dual-powder recoater with an in situ powder-mixing function has been demonstrated to produce vertical functionally graded (Ti6Al4V/In718) structures, indicating one route toward gradient interfaces. However, blade-based spreading as described cannot deposit different powders within the same build layer (i.e., lateral material patterning) — a key gap for high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery that requires rapid, spatially-resolved dosing and mixing. The text also notes L-PBF uses very fine powders, small laser spots, and thin layers (powder: 10–50 μm; spot: 50–80 μm; layer thickness: <100 μm), implying stringent requirements on powder-dosing precision and homogeneity for multi-material dispensing. Overall, the excerpt highlights existing solutions (dual containers, blade recoaters, in situ mixing modification) and their limits (no same-layer multi-powder deposition, sharp interfaces), which are critical challenges for automated, high-throughput multi-material alloy exploration.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies multiple challenges and gaps relevant to powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material AM alloy discovery. Key challenges include powder cross-contamination (worse at higher deposition rates), layer-bonding difficulties between different materials, powder deposition inaccuracies, and the need to adapt process parameters locally at material transition zones. Data-preparation limitations are important: coater-based MM workflows use separate STL files per volumetric section and nozzle concepts require bespoke CNC code, making data prep error-prone and preventing efficient graded-material definitions; advanced volumetric data formats (AMF, 3MF, FAV, SVX) are available but not widely implemented. Post-process powder reconditioning is problematic because cross-contamination forces sorting; manual ferromagnetic separation showed highly variable purities, automated sorting performed worse, while sieving and sedimentation delivered good purities for some cases (e.g., sieving >97.6% part.%, sedimentation separable for W vs Al/Ti). Demonstrated deposition/dosing concepts include holohedral, nozzle-based, and pressure-based masked deposition, but contamination levels (e.g., 14–40 wt.% for holohedral overflows) and device-dependent contamination have not been fully characterized. Monitoring methods (active thermography, CCD, spectrometers/photodetectors) can detect contaminants, and initial ML approaches exist for predicting scanning parameters, but comprehensive automated mixing/dosing systems and standardized solutions for high-throughput, low-contamination multi-material metal AM remain limited.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies key challenges for multi-material powder dispensing in L-PBF: unmelted powder retention in the bed causes cross-contamination when attempting to deposit dissimilar powders in the same layer, necessitating cleaning between depositions; blade-based recoaters cannot reliably deposit different powders on the same layer and repeated scraping can damage soft blade tips, degrading surfaces. Ultrasonic dispensing offers micron-scale feed precision and patterning capability but is limited by very low point-by-point throughput and requires improvement in layer thickness uniformity. Hybrid approaches (blade + ultrasonic) combine a main blade spread with ultrasonic dispensers for small-volume materials and employ micro-vacuum suction to remove excess unmelted powder to reduce contamination. Engineering fixes to ultrasonic dispensers include adding a miniature vibration motor to stabilize powder flow and creating arrays of dispensers (six-unit arrays) to print spatial FGMs. Electrophotographic/electrostatic methods (Aerosint dual-toner selective spreading) and dual-powder recoater concepts have been demonstrated for complex SS–copper patterns. These represent automated powder dosing/multiplexing strategies, but throughput, cross-contamination control, and uniform layer formation remain gaps for high-throughput alloy discovery workflows.
contexts.question: What are the challenges and gaps in powder dispensing for high-throughput multi-material additive manufacturing alloy discovery? What automated powder mixing and dosing solutions exist for metal AM research?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports use of the Freeman Technology FT4 as one of three shear-cell-style devices (RST, PFT, FT4) to determine AIFE (effective angles related to internal friction) for metal powders. FT4 produced the largest variations in measured AIFE values among the instruments tested, likely reflecting methodological differences. The FT4 measurement involves an impeller (shear head) that penetrates the entire powder bed and during standardised sample preparation the powder bed is loosened as part of the measurement. The text notes a 1 mm clearance between the glass cylinder and the measuring impeller where larger particles (e.g., titanium) can wedge, increasing resistance and shear stress—indicating FT4 sensitivity to particle size and shape. SEMs correlated particle morphology (spherical, oval, smooth vs. complex shapes) with AIFE outcomes across instruments including FT4, and powders were grouped into lower/middle/higher AIFE areas (e.g., 316L, Zn, Sn in the lower area). Overall, the excerpt highlights FT4's role in AIFE measurement, its sensitivity to particle geometry and size effects, and that its measurement method can cause powder bed loosening and greater spread in reported angles.
contexts.question: What are the specifications and capabilities of the Freeman Technology FT4 powder rheometer for metal powder characterization in additive manufacturing?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes how the Freeman Technology FT4 powder rheometer prepares and measures metal powder samples: an impeller penetrates through the entire powder bed during sample preparation, loosening (aerating) and standardizing the sample before shear measurements. This built-in loosening/aeration step can lead FT4 to report smaller AIFE (angle of internal friction equivalent) values for some powders (e.g., Zn: FT4 AIFE 26.5° ±0.4) but larger values for others (e.g., Ti: FT4 AIFE 45.6° ±1.9), likely influenced by cell design differences. The FT4 provides AIFE and flow index (ffc) outputs comparable to PFT and RST devices; according to ffc the FT4 classifies many tested metal powders as free-flowing and produces similar flow-mode assignments to PFT. However, the excerpt notes larger variance for certain materials (notably titanium and molybdenum), indicating FT4 may be less suitable or less stable for those powders, and it cautions that ffc sensitivity alone may be insufficient for distinguishing flow regimes. Overall, FT4 capability highlights sample aeration/standardization, shear/ AIFE measurement, and ffc-based flow classification, with material-dependent variability.
contexts.question: What are the specifications and capabilities of the Freeman Technology FT4 powder rheometer for metal powder characterization in additive manufacturing?

contexts.context: The FT4 (Freeman Technology, Tewkesbury, UK) rotary shear module is used to measure friction parameters and flowability (AIFE and flow index ffc) of metal powders. Its measurement principle uses a vessel containing the sample and a shear head whose blades sink into the powder; the head applies normal stress, moves downward until a stable pressure is achieved, then rotates slowly to induce shear. A shear plane forms just below the blade ends; because the powder bed resists rotation, shear stress in the measuring plane rises until slippage occurs, and the maximum transferred shear stress at slippage is recorded. In this study AIFE was measured at a consolidated normal stress of 10,000 Pa and the FT4 software was used to derive the flow index ffc. Compared with the other shear cells used, the FT4 had the smallest shear cell area (1879 mm2) but the highest bed level (44.5 mm), indicating differences in sample volume and geometry that can affect measured shear strength and ffc.

snippets: The third measurement of the AIFE resp. flowability evaluation was performed on the FT4 (Freeman Technology, Tewkesbury, Gloucestershire, UK).
The rotary shear module for measuring friction parameters consists of a vessel containing the sample powder and a shear head to cause normal and shear ​stress50.
The blades of the shear head sink into the mass powder and the front face of the head starts to apply normal stress to the surface of the powder bed.
The shear head moves downwards until a sufficient and stable pressure is applied between the head and powder bed.
Then the shear head starts to rotate slowly and thus cause shear stress within the bulk mass.
The shear plane is formed just below the end of the blades.
Since the powder bed prevents the rotation of the shear head, the shear stress in the measuring plane increases until slippage occurs.
Then, the maximum value of transferred shear stress is recorded.
The AIFE was again measured for standard (consolidated) stress—10,000 Pa.
The software relate to device was used to derive the flow index ffc.
RST has the largest shear cell area (8482 ​mm2), followed by PFT (4750 ​mm2) and finally FT4 (1879 ​mm2).
The level of the bed in the shear cell was highest for FT4 (44.5 mm), then RST (19.3 mm), and finally for PFT (9 mm).

contexts.context: The FT4 powder rheometer (Freeman Technology) is presented as a more sensitive, instrumented method than funnel tests for detailed powder rheology in metal AM. The study used five FT4 testing modes: dynamic flow, aeration, permeability, compressibility, and shear. Dynamic flow measures torque from a rotating blade in a filled vessel and reports metrics including basic flow energy (BFE, energy in the 7th downward repetition), conditioned bulk density (ρc), and specific energy (SE, energy during upward traverse). The aeration test introduces air at varying velocities to a loosely packed bed and records resistance; software outputs include aeration energy (AE). Compressibility is measured with a vented piston applying increasing compressive load to get powder bed volume change and a compressibility index (CI = compressed density / ρc). The permeability test introduces air at the column base under increasing compressive loads, monitoring air pressure to calculate pressure drop (PD). Shear testing evaluates stresses needed to initiate flow under consolidation and yields metrics such as the cohesion coefficient (c). Measurements were replicated three times with averages and standard deviations reported. Manufacturer documentation is referenced for further FT4 outputs and operational details.

snippets: An FT4 powder rheometer, being more sensitive than the rather simplistic funnel-based techniques, is intended to provide a finer evaluation of the rheological behavior of powders.
For this study, five testing modes were selected, namely dynamic flow, aeration, permeability, compressibility, and shear testing modes.
In the dynamic flow test, a vessel is filled with powder and the torque applied by the rotating blade moving through the powder bed is recorded.
the basic flow energy (BFE), which represents the energy recorded in the seventh repetition during the downward motion (confined motion)
the conditioned bulk density (ρc), which represents a bulk density of the conditioned powder bed
the specific energy (SE), which represents the energy recorded during the upward traverse of the blade (unconfined motion).
The FT4 powder aeration test allows the measurement of the flow energy of a loosely packed powder bed by introducing air at different velocities and recording the resistance to the blade rotation.
The software outputs include the aeration energy (AE).
Powder compressibility is evaluated by applying an increasing compressive load with a vented piston to evaluate the powder bed volume change as a function of the applied force.
A compressibility index (CI) is generated by dividing the compressed powder bed density by the conditioned bulk density (ρc) measured prior to start of the compression cycle.
The FT4 permeability test measures the ease with which a powder bed releases entrapped air.
Air is introduced at the bottom of the powder column and increasing levels of compressive force are applied with a vented piston.
the pressure drop (PD) is calculated.
The FT4 powder rheometer allows also the evaluation of shear stresses required to initiate the powder flow under consolidation.
As an output, several metrics of interest can be extracted, one of them being the cohesion coefficient (c).
For each rheological assessment mode presented above, the measurements were replicated three times using fresh samples of each powder, and the average and standard deviation values were calculated.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports several FT4-specific measurement details relevant to metal powder characterization. FT4 produced, in most cases, the lowest flow factor of cohesion (ffc) among the compared instruments; exceptions were Zn and Mo. The reported slip-surface (shear cross-section) area for FT4 is 1879 mm2 (smaller than PFT and RST), and the FT4 specimen column height used here was 44.5 mm (RST 19.3 mm, PFT 9 mm). These geometry differences correlate with FT4 giving the highest measured unconfined yield strength (σc) for the same normal loads, indicating that a smaller shear cross-section and higher column increase σc and reduce apparent flowability. Tests were run at normal loads of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 kPa; differing σc values are required when ffc differs across devices even with the same normal loads. The authors note that ffc depends on slip-surface size and can be affected by machine settings (e.g., set normal tension) and particle deformation. They also caution that the flow index’s sensitivity alone may be insufficient to classify flow regimes, and other particle properties (size, shape, roughness) and instrument parameters influence results.

snippets: In most cases, the lowest ffc values are obtained for the FT4. The exceptions are Zn and Mo powder.
The ffc values are probably related to the size of the slip surface, which is the largest in RST (8482 ­mm2), then for the PFT (4750 ­mm2) and finally the FT4 (1879 ­mm2).
If the same normal loads were used for the RST, PFT and FT4 measurements, and ffc came out differently, as a rule, different σc strengths had to be achieved.
The highest σc unconfined yield strength was achieved with the FT4.

Since the largest cell volume was in RST and the smallest in PFT, the σc strength had to be related to the material height, which was highest in FT4 (44.5 mm), followed by RST (19.3 mm), and PFT (9 mm).
All metal powders were tested at normal load 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 kPa.
It can therefore be attributed to the machine settings and it must be mentioned that in these cases the deformation of the particles can also have an effect.

As can be seen, according to ffc, Mn powder, Bronze powder, and also Mo powder, belong to the easy-flowing group, i.e. to a regime that is having a flow rate that is worse by a degree than all other tested metal powders.
However, it is evident that the sensitivity of the flow index itself to be included in flow regimes is not entirely sufficient.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Aerosint’s selective powder deposition approach as a drum-based recoater system for multimetal PBF. The system relies on purely mechanical forces to hold powder on the drum and then release it at desired locations, making it a selective-deposition method with similarities to printing technologies. The Aerosint unit is offered as an optional depositon system for the Aconity3D midi+ modular L-PBF platform, and the company is developing a quadruple deposition unit to increase material options. The excerpt does not provide any quantitative specifications — no numbers for resolution, layer deposition rate, throughput, supported particle size ranges, achievable packing density, or pricing/availability. To obtain specification, resolution, deposition-rate, and price details one would need to consult Aerosint product datasheets, Aconity3D option documentation, or contact the vendors directly, since the present text only establishes the technology type and platform integration without technical or commercial figures.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the working principles and some qualitative capabilities of the Aerosint selective powder deposition recoater. The system uses mechanical forces to hold powder on a drum and release it at chosen locations; each drum deposits one material and can generate a 2D single-material image in a line-by-line manner. Resolution is stated to be directly related to the number and spacing of actuators, while layer thickness is controlled by the applied layer height on the drum and the drum rotation vs. translation speeds. Multi-material capability is achieved by adding dedicated drum and actuator assemblies per material; scaling beyond two materials increases technical complexity and cost. The text notes commercial availability of a dual-material Aerosint recoater and anticipates further development (triple/quad) but does not provide numeric specifications, deposition rate figures, resolution in microns, or pricing. Therefore the excerpt is useful for understanding mechanism, scaling considerations, and the qualitative factors that determine resolution and layer thickness, but lacks the quantitative specs, rates, and price information needed to fully answer the question.
contexts.question: What are the Aerosint selective powder deposition recoater specifications, resolution, deposition rate, and price for multi-material powder bed fusion?

contexts.context: The paper reports on a robot-guided, nozzle-based powder deposition approach for multi-material PBF-LB. Nozzle methods trade build speed for selective deposition and lower cross-contamination; reported volumetric flow/build rates for nozzle approaches are about 0.4–4.1 mm3 s−1. Demonstrated nozzle/dispensing implementations in the literature include piezo-driven ultrasonic needle dispensers (examples with needle diameters 0.3–0.66 mm giving controllable flow for 316L) and a dual-vibration (ultrasonic + mini-motor) system using a 0.8 mm inner-diameter needle for silicon carbide powder. Such vibration-assisted dry-powder deliveries and fluidization/fluid-assisted methods are commonly used to overcome limited flowability. Resolution (localized deposition width) is governed by nozzle/orifice size and powder track behavior; the study evaluates single- and multi-track depositions and track widths but does not list numeric track-widths in the excerpt. The authors implemented a two-nozzle system integrated into an AMCM M 290-1 machine guided by an ABB IRB1090 robot to selectively deposit copper and ceramic powders (with aluminum applied via the conventional recoater), demonstrating practical multi-material capabilities for three-material parts (e.g., battery cell caps).
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes nozzle-based (pipette/pipet) powder deposition approaches for multimetal powder-bed fusion. Demonstrated nozzle-based systems include piezo-driven pipet dosing (Manchester University / Chao Wei’s work), vibration-driven pipets, pipet dosing augmented with an integrated vacuum to locally remove powder, and suction-enabled pipet replacements used in a two-step (recoating + local replacement) workflow akin to IGCV. Strengths: pipet dosing is versatile and scalable (adding pipettes enables more materials) and has been used for metal–metal and for glass/metal/ceramic composite coprocessing. Limitations: nozzle diameter required for reliable powder flow constrains the achievable resolution; point-by-point deposition is slow for filling whole beds; suction-depth control can remove more material than desired; vibration-driven pipets can produce low bed density (research into in-process precompaction is ongoing). The excerpt also cites selective-deposition alternatives: Sintef’s Metal Printing Process (MPP) based on electrostatics and Aerosint’s drum-based selective powder deposition (commercialized and integrable with Aconity3D systems). The text does not provide quantitative deposition rates or numeric resolution values.
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes powder bed fusion (PBF) as a thermal-energy-driven AM family (including SLS and SLM) that can use laser or electron beams (LPBF, EBPBF). It notes PBF's material versatility (polymers, ceramics, metals) and common drawbacks such as accuracy limits from particle size, powder reuse issues, and need for controlled atmosphere. The text explicitly references a "point-by-point powder delivery-based PBF process" (and a dispenser in the figure), indicating nozzle-style, localized powder delivery approaches have been demonstrated. Specific multi-material demonstrations cited include bi-metallic 316L steel + CuSn10 bronze via multi-material SLM, TiB2/Ti6Al4V metal–ceramic multi-materials, and metal–glass parts combining 316L steel and soda lime using the point-by-point powder delivery approach. However, the excerpt does not provide numeric data on nozzle-based powder deposition rates, voxel/feature resolution, or detailed descriptions (manufacturer names or system models) of nozzle deposition hardware. Additional sources or other sections of the paper would be needed to obtain quantitative deposition-rate and resolution specifications and explicit deposition-system names.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses selective powder-deposition approaches for powder-bed AM rather than explicit nozzle-based ‘‘nozzle’’ systems. It describes drum-based selective deposition (Aerosint) where each drum deposits a single material to produce 2D material images line-by-line; layer thickness is controlled by drum-layer height and drum/translation speeds. Resolution is linked to actuator count and spacing, and repeatability can be ensured after calibration. Aerosint offers a commercial dual-material recoater and is developing a quadruple-deposition unit with Fraunhofer IGCV and Vectoflow, showing scalable multi-material capability by adding dedicated drums/actuators. Pipet-based systems (Iro3D, Grid-Logic) deposit casting sand and metal powder into 3D powder structures but have coarser resolution due to pipette bores “in the mm range.” Sintef explored using a non-sintering support material and concepts for near-net-shape single-material parts. 3D Systems announced a selective powder-deposition approach to limit in-build powder and reported first prints. The excerpt does not provide quantitative nozzle-based deposition rates nor numeric resolution metrics for nozzle systems; it mainly documents demonstrated systems (Aerosint, Sintef, Iro3D, Grid-Logic, 3D Systems) and qualitative notes on resolution and multi-material scaling.
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

contexts.context: The excerpt notes that conventional metal powder bed fusion (PBF) processes are generally limited to single-material builds and "simply cannot easily accomplish multi-material processing due to the characteristics of the process." It contrasts PBF with direct deposition routes: powder-based direct deposition is highlighted as better suited for intricate, multi-material metallic structures because reducing feedstock from wire to powder increases packing density and resolution. Quantitative capability given for powder-based direct deposition is "dimensional accuracy at approximately 25–130 μm and a surface roughness of 1–2 μm." The text lists common metal AM processes (DMLM/DMLF, EBM, LENS, DLD/DED/DMD, LMWD, EBF/EBAM) and explains wire-fed processes can readily change feedstock mid-build ("the wire being fed under the beam can be easily changed during the process to make a multi-material part"); compositionally graded parts can be produced by combining different wires or combining wire with metallic powders in a single build. However, the excerpt does not provide nozzle-based powder deposition rates, detailed nozzle resolutions, nor list specific nozzle deposition systems for PBF nozzle-based powder delivery.
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

snippets: For example, metal powder bed fusion processes, though reliable and widely accepted in both academia and industry, simply cannot easily accomplish multi-material processing due to the characteristics of the process and are therefore limited to single material usage.

Powder-based direct deposition techniques provide the best fit for the two requirements necessary to fabricate intricate metallic structures, with dimensional accuracy at approximately 25–130 μm and a surface roughness of 1–2 μm.
Reducing the material from wire form to powder form is the easiest way to achieve this as packing density increases and a scaled-down material generally equates to scaled-down resolution.

direct metal laser melting/fusion (DMLM/DMLF), electron beam melting (EBM), laser engineering net shaping (LENS), direct laser/energy/metal deposition (DLD/DED/DMD), laser-based metal wire deposition (LMWD), and electron beam freeform fabrication (EBF) (or electron beam additive manufacturing (EBAM)).
the wire being fed under the beam can be easily changed during the process to make a multi-material part.

From this selection, compositionally-graded parts can be built from a large variation of materials by combining different wires or even by combining wire with metallic powders from a separate feed during a single build [64,65].
It is partially because of this capability that powder-based direct deposition is making large strides toward innovation when compared to wire fed direct deposition [66].

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the landscape of nozzle- and selective-powder deposition approaches for multimetal laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF). It notes that a few research groups developed systems for selective deposition of multiple powders in a single powder layer and that each group implemented its own multimetal solution. Demonstrated deposition concepts referenced include ultrasonic material dispensing–based selective laser melting, in situ powder mixing for functionally graded metal–glass components, electrostatic particle application modules, and commercial/industrial systems or flyers (Aconity3D Midi+). The excerpt also cites patents and disclosures for selective powder delivery and coater apparatus (WO 2019/074955 A1; US 2017/0057013 A1; US 9,604,411 B2) and company/industry posts (Aerosint). However, the excerpt does not provide quantitative nozzle-based deposition rates or detailed resolution metrics; those specific performance numbers are not present here and would need to be obtained from the cited papers, patent filings, or vendor datasheets.
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

contexts.context: The excerpt categorizes three powder-deposition approaches for 3D multi-material (3D-MM) PBF: holohedral, nozzle-based, and masked. It provides quantitative performance for a suction-based holohedral/multi-chamber system (Anstaett et al.) with an estimated deposition rate of about 60–140 mm3/s versus 240–560 mm3/s for mono-material PBF-LB. Resolution is reported as laser-limited: minimum feature sizes ~130–240 µm plus nominal overlaps of 100–200 µm at 30 µm layer height; parts below 1×1×1 mm3 have been achieved in some alloys. The nozzle-based family is noted to date to the early 1990s and typically relies on interparticular forces with actuators that fluidize powder and deposit it through a nozzle, but this excerpt does not give explicit deposition-rate numbers, detailed resolutions, or a list of demonstrated nozzle-specific systems. The excerpt also describes a multi-material suction/coating workflow requiring at least four coater passes per layer to enable 3D-MM transitions.
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes material-spreading approaches used for multi-material laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF). A blade-based dual-powder recoater that uses two powder containers has been demonstrated to produce vertical bimetallic builds (example: copper–stainless steel) but results in sharp material interfaces that can cause high stress concentrations. An improved blade-based recoater with in situ powder mixing was shown (Scaramuccia et al., 2020) to produce a vertical Ti6Al4V/In718 functionally graded material (FGM). However, blade-based spreading as described cannot deposit different powders within the same build layer, limiting some multi-material configurations. The paper also provides typical L-PBF resolution-related process ranges: powder sizes ~10–50 μm (for L-PBF), laser spot diameters ~50–80 μm, and layer thicknesses <100 μm, which indicate the achievable geometrical resolution for L-PBF. The excerpt does not provide nozzle-based powder deposition rates or specific nozzle-system demonstrations; it focuses on blade-based and in situ mixing recoater systems and highlights the remaining challenge of within-layer dissimilar powder deposition.
contexts.question: What are the nozzle-based powder deposition rates, resolution, and multi-material capabilities for powder bed fusion AM? What specific deposition systems have been demonstrated?

contexts.context: The provided excerpt contains Table 6 from Jiang et al. (2023), which compares percentage dispensing error for three dispensing platforms (Quantos, a dual‑arm robot, and Chemspeed) across thirteen solid powders at four target weights (1000, 500, 200, 20 mg). The table lists the powder types tested (examples in the table include Al2O3, NaNO2, Granulated sugar, Molecular sieve and others) and the percent errors for each device and target weight. Percent error entries for the Quantos are given (for example, Al2O3 at 1000 mg: −0.02% ± 0.05%; NaNO2 at 1000 mg: 0.16% ± 0.17%), which can be converted to absolute mass error by multiplying the percent error by the target mass (e.g., −0.02% of 1000 mg ≈ −0.2 mg; 0.16% of 1000 mg ≈ 1.6 mg). The excerpt also notes dispensing problems “typically caused by a compressible solid clogging up the dispensing,” but it does not include a description of the Quantos dispensing mechanism (e.g., dosing principle), manufacturer technical specifications, or explicit Chemspeed GDU‑P specifications or throughput. To fully answer the question, manufacturer datasheets or other sections of the paper (not present in this excerpt) will be needed.
contexts.question: What is the Mettler Toledo Quantos dispensing mechanism, accuracy in milligrams, and what powder types does it work with? What are the Chemspeed GDU-P specifications and throughput?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes use of a Mettler Toledo Quantos handheld powder dosing system (model HPD) with an orifice size of 2.5/4 mm, used alongside a Mettler Toledo microbalance MT5 in a master’s project to fill single capsules. The authors report that the HPD Quantos "can be part of a method for preparing individual solid dosage forms, the equivalent of one-fifth of a tablet." They also state "The handheld version of Quantos is not accurate enough for small capsule sizes." Materials tested included crushed Allopurinol Teva 100 mg tablets and Cellets microcrystalline cellulose pellets; various Coni-snap capsule sizes were screened. The excerpt does not provide a numerical accuracy in milligrams, a detailed dispensing mechanism beyond the device name and orifice size, nor any specification or throughput data for a Chemspeed GDU-P—those details are not present in the provided text.
contexts.question: What is the Mettler Toledo Quantos dispensing mechanism, accuracy in milligrams, and what powder types does it work with? What are the Chemspeed GDU-P specifications and throughput?

contexts.context: Relevant details in the excerpt about the Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5: its dosing head uses an embedded RFID chip and a learning/adaptive algorithm during initial dispenses to optimize accuracy and speed, and the dispense head employs a combination of rotary and tapping actions. The study evaluated target masses of 2, 10, and 50 mg and reports that % error improved as target mass increased. The algorithm learned dosing parameters quickly for powders such as D-mannitol, L-proline, PVPP and thiamine HCl, while fumed silica, limestone, and sodium chloride required several dispenses for optimization. The excerpt does not provide explicit accuracy values in milligrams or absolute mg error figures. About Chemspeed: the text describes Chemspeed SWING systems having an optional “Teaching” operation that performs initial dispenses to set parameters, that all SWING dispenses were performed in a purge box environment, and that fumed silica could not be dispensed at the 50 mg target due to very low bulk density. The excerpt does not contain specific Chemspeed GDU-P specifications or throughput numbers.
contexts.question: What is the Mettler Toledo Quantos dispensing mechanism, accuracy in milligrams, and what powder types does it work with? What are the Chemspeed GDU-P specifications and throughput?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that the Mettler-Toledo Quantos platform (listed as Mettler-Toledo Quantos QB5) uses both rotary and tapping actions to facilitate powder flow. The study evaluated dispensing at target masses of 2, 10, and 50 mg (with fumed silica only at 2 and 10 mg) and recorded mass dispensed (mg), dispense time (sec), and % error relative to target mass. Seven powders were tested across systems: D-mannitol, fumed silica (Aerosil 200), L-proline, limestone powder (BCR reference), polyvinylpolypyrrolidone (PVPP, Kollidon CL), sodium chloride (Redi-Dri), and thiamine HCl. Table 3 shows the Quantos column (QB5) with high numbers/retention (e.g., 680 dispenses at 100% retained for D-mannitol), indicating the Quantos successfully dispensed most powders in this study. However, the excerpt does not provide explicit numerical accuracy values in milligrams for Quantos (only the study's % error definition and target masses), nor does it include any specifications or throughput numbers for the Chemspeed GDU-P. contexts.context: The excerpt reports performance observations for the Mettler Toledo Quantos and the Chemspeed GDU-family (referred to as Chemspeed (GDU-Access Pfd) / GDU) in a comparative dispensing study. Quantos achieves <2% percentage error on non-challenging solids at a 200 mg target (Table 5 shows Quantos non-challenging solids around −0.41% with a 1.80% value shown), but certain powders are problematic: potassium acetate, molecular sieves, ammonium acetate, and highly abrasive materials such as silicon carbide/sand can cause major issues and even irreparable damage to Quantos dispensing heads. Failures often stem from dispensing-mechanism blockages. Table 4 (sodium nitrite timings) shows Quantos is relatively fast for larger targets (e.g., the table row includes "1000 ... 40   4" and "200 ... 31   3" suggesting ~40 s for 1000 mg and ~31 s for 200 mg in the Quantos column), but performance depends strongly on material, target weight and tolerance. The Chemspeed GDU is described as using a dispensing cartridge / dosing head; it experienced failures with some powders (e.g., poor performance with sugar) and mechanical failures related to closing/opening of the dispensing cartridge. Chemspeed timing entries in Table 4 are e.g. "1000 ... 134   17", "200 ... 109   19", "20 ... 62   23" indicating different throughput characteristics by target mass. Overall, the excerpt gives accuracy (percent error) and timing/throughput data and lists powder types that work well versus those that are challenging for each platform.

contexts.context: The HT-READ approach is an integrated high-throughput alloy development pipeline combining additive manufacturing (DED), computational screening (e.g., CALPHAD), a 16-sample ring library, and automated characterization to rapidly fabricate and test many alloy compositions. For combinatorial powder handling the demonstrated workflow uses pre-mixed powders: alloy powders (In625 and alloying elements) and a 200 ppm alumina flow aid are weighed, blended on a rolling mill, and loaded into designated vials in a Formalloy Alloy Development Feeder (ADF). The ADF contains 16 separate vials that rotate under computer control so one vial feeds the DED nozzle at a time, enabling up to 16 distinct compositions per build; vials are re-filled with new pre-mixed powders for each subsequent 16-ring sample. The paper notes alternative in-situ powder-mixing strategies that meter elemental powder feeds from repositories, but cautions these methods face significant hurdles when expanding beyond 3–4 elements. Consequently the authors suggest automating powder weighing, mixing, and vial filling to maintain composition fidelity for higher-component combinatorial libraries.
contexts.question: What is the HT-READ (high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development) approach and how does it handle powder mixing and dispensing for combinatorial alloy libraries?

snippets: We have presented an integrated high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (HT-READ) process, leveraging additive manufacturing synthesis, computational screening, and a novel sample library design.
Our 16-sample ring library facilitates rapid sample fabrication and automated characterization of phases, microstructures, and properties of structural alloys.
in additive manufacturing synthesis with a revolving powder feeder, vials are loaded with 16 pre-mixed alloy powders, and re-filled with new alloy powders for each new 16-ring sample.
recent works have achieved in-situ powder mixing, by utilizing repositories of individual elemental powders calibrated to flow powder at a rate consistent with desired sample compositions [23,46].
expanding the technique beyond 3-4 different elements will face significant hurdles for tuning compositions in higher component spaces.
It may be more practical to automate powder weighing and mixing, and subsequent vial filling, in order to retain the composition fidelity needed for complex alloy development.
In625 powder, alloying elements, and flow-aid powder were weighed to achieve desired compositions and blended on a rolling mill before being loaded into designated vials in the DED machine.
200ppm of an alumina-based flow aid powder (Aeroxide Alu-C, Evonik) was added to each composition to increase flowability through the additive powder delivery system.

This powder feeder contains 16 separate vials, designed to rotate via computer control, with one vial feeding into the DED system at a time, allowing for up to 16 distinct alloy compositions within one build cycle.
Samples were individually printed at 22.5-degree angles to one another using a Formalloy Directed Energy Deposition (DED) machine equipped with a 150W 450nm laser produced by Nuburu.

contexts.context: The paper describes a high-throughput, in situ alloying approach using directed energy deposition (DED) on a LENS MR-7 to produce discrete combinatorial libraries of bulk (3D) alloy samples on a single build plate. Composition variation is achieved by using four independently controlled powder hoppers whose powders are fed by augers into a common argon gas line. Powders are aggregated in the central gas line and mixed by turbulent gas flow during transit to the printhead; the mixed powder is then sprayed through four nozzles into a laser-induced melt pool where alloying occurs. This setup enables printing of different HEA compositions as arrays (not mere thin gradients), facilitating subsequent high-throughput SEM/EDS/XRD characterization while samples remain on the build plate. The described powder feeding and mixing strategy (independent hopper control + central mixing in gas flow) is the mechanism by which combinatorial compositions are dispensed and varied across the sample array.
contexts.question: What is the HT-READ (high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development) approach and how does it handle powder mixing and dispensing for combinatorial alloy libraries?

snippets: in situ alloying through additive manufacturing was employed to produce arrays of different HEA compositions.
printing of different HEA compositions, in 3D-component arrays, on a single build plate.
The LENS MR-7 is a powder-based metals 3D printer operating in a closed chamber filled with argon and fed by four independently controlled powder hoppers.
Powders from each hopper are drawn into a gas line of flowing argon by the rotation of an auger located at the base of each hopper.
These powders are aggregated in a central gas line and consequently mixed by the turbulent gas flow during transit to the printhead.

At the printhead, the mixed powder is sprayed out by four nozzles, with rotational symmetry about the optic axis of the printhead, where it encounters a laser impinging on the surface of the build plate.

The laser, with an approximate spot size of 600 μm and focal point ~380 μm below the surface, forms a melt pool on the surface of the build plate where incoming powder heated by the laser becomes incorporated.

To date, most high-throughput alloy synthesis techniques rely on producing compositional gradients ... This work seeks to extend high-throughput materials synthesis to bulk materials such that it may be coupled with existing high-throughput characterization and modeling techniques.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the HT-READ approach as an integrated, closed-loop high-throughput alloy development workflow that unifies computational screening (e.g., CALPHAD and machine learning), automated synthesis, processing, characterization, testing, and data analysis. An AI agent links compositions to properties and new experimental data can feed subsequent iterations. To enable automation and high-throughput characterization (XRD, SEM/EDS, EBSD), HT-READ uses miniaturized sample “libraries” rather than one-sample-at-a-time testing: a 32 mm metallurgical mount containing a rotationally symmetric ring of 16 discrete samples. The library is fabricated by 3D printing and each sample is uniquely identified and stored to maintain persistent sample-data linkage. The rotational geometry is chosen to simplify automation (favoring sample rotation over X-Y-Z translation) and to meet geometric constraints of electron- and x-ray-based techniques. The authors demonstrate the workflow using alloyed Inconel 625 as a model system. The provided excerpt does not describe any procedures or hardware for powder mixing or powder dispensing for combinatorial library fabrication.
contexts.question: What is the HT-READ (high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development) approach and how does it handle powder mixing and dispensing for combinatorial alloy libraries?

snippets: we developed a high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (HT-READ) methodology that comprises an integrated, closed-loop material screening process inspired by broad chemical assays and modern innovations in automation.
An artificial intelligence agent is used to find connections between compositions and material properties.
The sample libraries are assigned unique identifiers and stored to make data and samples persistent, thus preventing institutional knowledge loss.
The miniaturization and automation of experiments described herein begins with the synthesis of a sample “library,” as opposed to the traditional one-sample-at-a-time approach.
This sample library has been designed to fit in a single metallurgical mount (32mm diameter) and consists of a ring of 16 individual samples
The library of samples is fabricated using 3D printing

given the geometric constraints of these techniques, there are significant advantages of only employing sample rotations to position from one sample to the next, rather than positioning by X-Y-Z translations.
The ring configuration with 16 discrete samples allows for individual analysis of compositions while maintaining critical geometries necessary to multiple characterization techniques.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes both high-end and low-cost approaches for powder/solid dispensing in research HTE workflows. Commercial automated robots (Chemspeed SWAVE, Chemspeed Powderdose, Chemspeed Swile, Mettler Toledo Quantos, Unchained Labs Junior) and a reported custom automated robot provide accurate, low-RSD volumetric dosing (typical RSDs <5% in 96-well formats) but entail significant capital investment and lab space. Low-cost, broadly adoptable alternatives include calibrated fixed-volume and variable-volume scoops for reproducible volumetric manual dosing; after a single calibration step, scoops enable dispensing "without the use of a balance." A low-cost parallel ChemBead dispenser (three-plate design: top plate, metering plate with 96 calibrated holes for ~3 mg, and bottom plate) combines higher throughput with low maintenance and achieves consistent weight dispensing with an error margin of ±5%. These manual/parallel devices are presented as accessible, low-cost options that bridge the gap between full automation and purely manual weighing. The excerpt does not contain any pricing information or mention the Pellet dispensomixer or specific DIY powder-dosing costs.
contexts.question: What open-source or low-cost automated powder dispensing solutions exist for research laboratories? What are prices for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes two open-hardware, low-cost automated pellet/powder handling devices intended for lab-scale material compounding: the Pellet dispensomixer (dispenser) and a Pellet distributor (robotized tube arm). Both are Arduino-driven (UNO or MEGA as required) using stepper motors and A4988 drivers; control is via serial communication with a computer and the projects include Arduino code and Python notebooks (inverse kinematics via ikpy) in a public GitHub repository. All designs and CAD/STL files are provided for 3D printing, and the ESI includes a detailed Bill of Materials. Cost estimates given: the dispensomixer ~250 euros (~270 USD), an optional air ionizer used to mitigate static charges adds roughly 571 euros (~600 USD), the pellet distributor is quoted at ~150 euros (~160 USD) to build, and an electronic balance used in integration is ~285 euros (~300 USD). The device routines include motor-direction alternation to reduce blocking, and the systems are designed to integrate feedback from balances to control dispensing and container switching.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes an open-hardware approach for automating polymer compounding focused on two devices: a multi-material pellet dispensomixer and a pellet collector–distributor. The authors position this work as “open laboratory hardware” to automate material input to extruders and facilitate changing compositions during loading. It notes that dispensing solids is especially challenging due to particle size, shape and electrostatics, and cites prior work (M. N. Bahr) on high-throughput automated powder dispensing. The excerpt contrasts expensive commercial lab-scale powder dispensers (examples given: FLEX Powderdose, Chemspeed SWING GDU-Pfd, Mettler Toledo CHRONECT Quantos) with DIY/open solutions built on common microcontrollers and SBCs — “computers such as Arduino, Raspberry Pi and others” — and mentions adapted pipettes/liquid handlers as low-cost precedents. The paper’s devices aim to simplify preparation of targeted compositions from master batches and to integrate into automated workflows. The excerpt does not contain any pricing or specific cost figures for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing solutions.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes both commercial and open/DIY powder/pellet dispensing solutions. Commercial laboratory systems mentioned include FLEX Powderdose, Chemspeed SWING GDU-Pfd and Mettler Toledo CHRONECT Quantos. The authors situate their work within a growing open-source and DIY laboratory equipment movement, noting DIY setups can offer "enhanced customization at prices that can be around 94% less than their commercial analogues." Examples of alternative approaches include a system by Y. Jiang et al. that handles both powder and pellets, biomimetic robot-arm approaches (spatula + balance), and designs based on rotors or auger mechanisms. The Pellet dispensomixer presented is specifically designed for pellet-form materials, inspired by the Open Source Food Pellet Dispenser, and is paired with a novel pellet distributor for laboratory compounding (claimed to have no predecessor in the field). Design choices favored rotor-with-holes solutions, with iterative features (semicircular holes, ramps, fillets, rotor shaking) to prevent clogging. Characterization data in the excerpt give an average pellet mass of 0.009184 g (SD 0.003366 g) and an estimated sampling error of about 0.29% for a 150 g sample. The excerpt does not list an explicit purchase/build price for the Pellet dispensomixer itself.
contexts.question: What open-source or low-cost automated powder dispensing solutions exist for research laboratories? What are prices for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing?

contexts.context: This excerpt describes an open-hardware “pellet dispensomixer/pellet distributor” intended for lab-scale automated compounding of polymer pellets and masterbatches. The authors report the device as low-cost: the estimated bill-of-materials cost is around 150 euros (~160 USD). The project provides code and examples via GitHub repositories for operation and customization. Performance metrics from a PLA + masterbatch test include a measured dispensing accuracy of 99.33% and a mass flow of 7.59 g min−1 (≈454.15 g h−1). The design uses interchangeable rotors to change pellet shape and flow; the upper pellet size is limited by rotor-hole diameter. The control strategy dispenses small iterative increments (e.g., 1 g steps for the least-abundant component) with balance feedback and corrections to avoid cumulative error. Critically, the device is explicitly not designed for powders: powders exhibit different aggregation mechanics and can obstruct moving parts. The paper notes that CAD modifications could adapt geometry, but warns about powder handling limitations. Safety advice (e.g., proper wiring and common ground for the Arduino/12 V supply) and repository resources are provided.
contexts.question: What open-source or low-cost automated powder dispensing solutions exist for research laboratories? What are prices for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a novel funnel-shaped, flexible robotic end-effector that integrates a single-sheet valve and a feedback loop with an external balance to enable controllable, incremental dispensing of powders at the milligram scale. Experimental tests with glass beads, monosodium glutamate, and titanium dioxide showed strong performance: 80% of trials were within ±2 mg and the maximum error observed was approximately 20 mg across a target range of 20 mg to 3 g. The system uses model-based flow prediction and online parameter identification to adapt to powder dynamics; the authors report improved accuracy and convergence speed compared to direct PID control. Prior work is cited: a soft conical end-effector could scoop powders but "could only release the entire scooped amount," while Jiang et al. developed a dual-arm robot with three spatulas achieving weighing accuracy as low as 2 mg over a 20–1000 mg range. The paper focuses on challenges of scooping and milligram-scale dispensing, adaptability to diverse containers/quantities, and the need for control strategies that handle complex powder flow. The excerpt does not discuss open-source projects, DIY solutions, or commercial prices (e.g., Pellet dispensomixer).
contexts.question: What open-source or low-cost automated powder dispensing solutions exist for research laboratories? What are prices for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing?

contexts.context: This excerpt (Jiang et al., Digital Discovery 2023) presents Table 6 which compares percentage dispensing error for thirteen different solids at multiple target weights using three dispensing approaches: Quantos, a dual‑arm robot (with spatula outlet), and a Chemspeed system. The table reports error percentages across many material/weight combinations and shows frequent failures for some materials (multiple entries of −100.00 indicating unsuccessful dispensing). The authors note that failures were "typically caused by a compressible solid clogging up the dispensing" outlet and that both the dual‑arm robot and the Quantos platform "were able to dispens" (i.e., successfully dispense) for many cases. Overall, the excerpt demonstrates that commercial platforms (Quantos, Chemspeed) and a robotic dual‑arm method were evaluated for accuracy and robustness, with performance varying substantially by material and target mass. The excerpt does not provide information on open‑source or low‑cost DIY solutions, nor does it include pricing or details about the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing hardware.

contexts.context: This excerpt describes an automated solid-dispensing platform based on a highly dexterous dual-arm robotic manipulator that mimics a scientist using a spatula and analytical balance. The system uses a fuzzy-logic controller to choose motion parameters and manipulate various spatula sizes, transferring milligram to gram quantities. It reportedly handles a broad range of materials, including those that abrade or block moving-part dispensers, and can reset and re-dispense if weight exceeds a tolerance. The platform achieved dispensing accuracy as low as 2 mg and is described as significantly cheaper than most commercial solid-dispensing platforms. The excerpt also summarizes commercial options (Chemspeed GDU-S SWILE, GDU-Pfd, Quantos, Chronect XPR) and their mechanisms—gravity plus mechanical agitation, piston plus glass capillary with internal balance, crescent valve, and spiral metal impellers. The text does not provide price points, open-source project names, or mention the Pellet dispensomixer or DIY powder-dosing systems, so additional sources are needed for cost figures and open-source/DIY options.
contexts.question: What open-source or low-cost automated powder dispensing solutions exist for research laboratories? What are prices for the Pellet dispensomixer or similar DIY powder dosing?
